text: The Record of the Ancestry of Jesus Christ, Son of David, Son of Abraham.
As Jesus left the temple, his disciples drew his attention to the grandeur of its buildings.
Abraham fathered Isaac, Isaac fathered Jacob, Jacob fathered Judah and his brothers.
But Jesus said to them, “Do you see all these things? Truly, I tell you, not one stone will remain upon another, until it all is torn down.”
Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar, Perez fathered Hezron, Hezron fathered Ram.
As he sat on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately and asked, “Tell us, when will these things happen, and what will be the sign of your coming and the end of the age?”
Ram fathered Amminadab, Amminadab fathered Nahshon, Nahshon fathered Salmon.
Jesus answered them, “Listen carefully that no one deceives you.
Salmon fathered Boaz by Rahab, Boaz fathered Obed by Ruth, Obed fathered Jesse.
For many will come in my name, claiming, ‘I am the Christ,’ and will deceive many.
Jesse fathered David the king. David fathered Solomon by the wife of Uriah.
You will hear of wars and rumors of wars. Do not be alarmed, for these things are inevitable, but they are not the sign of the end.
Solomon fathered Rehoboam, Rehoboam fathered Abijah, Abijah fathered Asa.
For nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. There will be famines, plagues, and earthquakes in various places.
Asa fathered Jehoshaphat, Jehoshaphat fathered Joram, Joram fathered Uzziah.
All these are just the beginning of birth pains.
Uzziah fathered Jotham, Jotham fathered Ahaz, Ahaz fathered Hezekiah.
Then they will hand you over to be persecuted and killed, and you will be hated by all nations because of my name.
Hezekiah fathered Manasseh, Manasseh fathered Amon, Amon fathered Josiah.
At that time, many will fall away, betray one another, and hate one another.
Josiah fathered Jeconiah and his brothers at the time of the Babylonian exile.
Many false prophets will arise and spread deception.
After the Babylonian exile, Jeconiah fathered Shealtiel, Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel.
Because lawlessness will abound, the love of many will grow cold.
Zerubbabel fathered Abiud, Abiud fathered Eliakim, Eliakim fathered Azor.
But the one who endures to the end will be saved.
Azor fathered Zadok, Zadok fathered Achim, Achim fathered Eliud.
And this gospel of the kingdom will be proclaimed in all the world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come.
Eliud fathered Eleazar, Eleazar fathered Matthan, Matthan fathered Jacob.
So when you see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by the prophet Daniel, standing in the holy place (left for the reader's discernment),

footnotes.content: The phrase “left for the reader's discernment” suggests that the reference to the “abomination of desolation,” spoken of by the prophet Daniel, is a prophetic sign requiring careful reflection. It likely refers to a specific historical event, such as the desecration of the temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 168 B.C. or the Roman destruction in 70 A.D., but it also hints at a broader, recurring fulfillment, encouraging each generation to discern its meaning in their own context.

text: Jacob fathered Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom Jesus was born, who is called Christ.
then let those in Judea flee to the mountains.
So from Abraham to David: fourteen generations; from David to the Babylonian exile: fourteen generations; and from the exile to Christ: fourteen generations.

footnotes.content: Matthew arranges the genealogy in three groups of 14 for mnemonic and symbolic reasons—14 is the numeric value of “David” in Hebrew (דוד = 4+6+4). He intentionally omits several kings (e.g., Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah in v. 8) to shape the structure, emphasizing Jesus as the promised Son of David and signaling divine design in Israel's history (cf. 2 Chronicles 22–25). This stylized list follows ancient Jewish literary practices for theological storytelling.

text: Let the one on the housetop not come down to take anything from the house.
This is how Jesus Christ was born: Before Mary and Joseph came together, she was found to be pregnant by the Holy Spirit.

footnotes.content: The phrase "from the Holy Spirit" translates the Greek ek pneumatos hagiou, which emphasizes origin rather than association. It points to the conception as truly originating in the Spirit's initiating action—not merely attributed to the Spirit (“of the Holy Spirit”) or performed as an external act (“by the Holy Spirit”), but proceeding directly from divine source.

text: And let the one in the field not turn back to get a cloak.
Joseph, being a righteous man and unwilling to disgrace her, resolved to send her away quietly.

footnotes.content: The term "disgrace" reflects the serious social and moral consequences in first-century Jewish culture, where public shame was devastating. The Greek deigmatisai implies public exposure, while "send her away quietly" translates apolusai, meaning to release or dismiss. Together, they reveal Joseph's dilemma and his desire to protect Mary's dignity with quiet compassion.

text: Grief upon those who are pregnant and nursing in those days!

footnotes.content: The phrase “Grief upon those who are pregnant and nursing in those days” emphasizes the extreme vulnerability and unavoidable suffering that such individuals will face during the coming tribulation. This is not a judgment against them personally, but a recognition of the harsh conditions that will make escape and survival particularly challenging for those burdened with the care of the most dependent. It captures the intensity of the crisis, when even the strongest natural bonds and instincts for protection will be strained to the breaking point.

text: As he pondered this, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream, saying, “Joseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mary as your wife; the child conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.”
Pray that your flight is not in winter or on the Sabbath.
She will bear a son, and you shall name him Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins.
For then there will be great tribulation, unmatched from the beginning of the world until now, and never to be repeated.
All this happened to fulfill what the Lord said through the prophet:
If those days were not cut short, no one would be saved, but for the sake of the chosen, those days will be shortened.
“Behold, the virgin will conceive and bear a son, and they will call him Immanuel” (meaning “God with us”).
Then if anyone tells you, ‘Look, here is the Christ!’ or ‘There he is!’ do not believe it.
When Joseph woke, he did as the angel of the Lord commanded and took Mary as his wife.
For false christs and false prophets will arise and perform great signs and wonders to deceive, if they could even reach the chosen.

footnotes.content: The phrase “if they could even reach the chosen” acknowledges that the deception will be so intense and persuasive that it could nearly mislead even those specifically chosen by God. This phrasing captures the severity of the spiritual threat without asserting that the chosen can actually be overcome, reflecting a divine safeguard over those called to endure. However, this does not imply immunity from testing; the chosen will still face extreme pressure, falsehoods, and trials designed to shake even the firmest faith. Their perseverance will be both a mark of divine preservation and a testament to their steadfast loyalty.

text: But he did not touch her until she gave birth to a Son, and he named him Jesus.

footnotes.content: The term “firstborn” is omitted here because it does not appear in the earliest and most reliable manuscripts. Its inclusion in later versions may introduce theological ambiguity by implying that Mary had additional children, which conflicts with the traditional doctrine of her perpetual virginity. However, the word “until” is retained, as it faithfully reflects the Greek heōs hou, which does not necessarily imply a change afterward. In Greek usage, this phrase marks the state of affairs up to a point in time without making any implication about what happened after. Its use here preserves the linguistic structure while avoiding speculative interpretation. Greek egnō means “to know” in the sense of sexual union. The traditional rendering “did not know her” was replaced with “did not touch her” to avoid ambiguity and improve clarity for modern readers, while retaining the poetic tone and biblical idiom.

text: Beware, for I have told you ahead of time.
So if they insist, ‘Look, he is in the wilderness,’ do not go out, or ‘Look, he is in the inner rooms,’ do not believe it.
For just as lightning comes from the east and shines to the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
Wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will circle.

title: The Coming of the Son of Man

text: Immediately after the tribulation of those days, the sun will be darkened, the moon will cease to shine, the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken.
Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky, and all the peoples of the earth will mourn, for they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.
And he will send out his angels with a mighty trumpet call, and they will gather his chosen from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other.

title: The Lesson of the Fig Tree

text: Learn this lesson from the fig tree: When its branch becomes tender and sprouts leaves, you know that summer is near.
So also, when you see all these things, know that it is near, right at the door.
Truly, I tell you, this age will not pass away until all these things have happened.
Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away.
But concerning that day and hour, no one knows; not the angels of heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.
For as it was in the days of Noah, so will it be at the coming of the Son of Man.
For in the days before the flood, people were eating and drinking, taking wives and giving daughters in marriage, until the day Noah entered the ark.
and they were unaware until the flood came and swept them all away; so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
Then two will be in the field; one will be taken, and the other left.
Two women will be grinding at the mill; one will be taken, and the other left.

footnotes.content: The image of “two women grinding at the mill” reflects a common, daily task in the ancient world, typically performed by women using a hand mill or stone quern. This phrase emphasizes the sudden, selective nature of judgment, where ordinary life is interrupted without warning, dividing even those engaged in the same work. It highlights the unpredictability of the coming separation and the need for constant readiness.

text: Therefore, stay alert, for you do not know on what day or hour your Lord will come.

footnotes.content: The phrase “on what day or hour” captures the full uncertainty of the timing, emphasizing that even those most vigilant cannot anticipate the exact moment. This reflects the broader biblical theme of spiritual vigilance, as seen in the parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25), where readiness is essential for those awaiting the bridegroom's return.

text: But understand this: if the master of the house had known at what time the thief would come, he would have stayed awake and not allowed his house to be broken into.
For this reason, you also must be ready, for the Son of Man is coming at an hour you do not expect.
Who then is the faithful and wise slave, whom the master has put in charge of the household to give them their food at the proper time?

footnotes.content: The choice of the term “slave” rather than “servant” reflects the deeper theological reality that those who follow Christ are considered bondservants or slaves to their master, Jesus. This aligns with the broader New Testament teaching that believers are purchased by Christ's sacrifice and belong fully to him, serving his will above their own. This concept is emphasized in passages like 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 (“You are not your own; you were bought with a price”) and Romans 6:22 (“But now, having been freed from sin and enslaved to God, you derive your benefit, resulting in sanctification, and the outcome, eternal life”). This terminology captures the full scope of devotion and obedience expected of true followers.

text: Blessed is that slave whom the master finds doing his will when he returns.
Truly, I tell you, they will be put in charge of all the master's possessions.
But if that wicked slave says to themselves, ‘My master is delayed,’
and begins to beat their fellow slaves and eat and drink with the drunkards,
the master of that slave will come on a day they do not expect and at an hour they do not know,
and will cut them to pieces and send them to their place with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.

footnotes.content: The phrase “send them to their place with the hypocrites” reflects the severe and final nature of the judgment described. The Greek here, meros, carries the sense of an appointed portion or rightful place, emphasizing that the wicked slave receives what is fitting for their unfaithfulness. This aligns with the broader biblical principle that those who betray their master's trust will face a just and severe recompense, often characterized by separation from the faithful and eternal regret, as seen in the repeated phrase “weeping and gnashing of teeth” throughout Matthew (e.g., 8:12, 13:42, 13:50, 22:13, 25:30).

text: After Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea during the days of King Herod, Magi from the east came to Jerusalem.

footnotes.content: Magi were Eastern court astrologer-priests (probably Persian). Retained here to distinguish them from generic “wise men” and to highlight Old Testament background (cf. Daniel 2).

text: They said, “Where is the one born King of the Jews? We saw his star at its rising and have come to worship him.”
When Herod heard this, he was troubled, and all Jerusalem with him.

footnotes.content: The phrase “so was all Jerusalem” is used instead of the more traditional “all Jerusalem with him” to avoid the misleading implication that the people were disturbed by the Christ's birth itself. The Greek phrase kai pasa Ierousalēm met' autou literally means “and all Jerusalem with him,” but the construction can also imply simultaneous consequence rather than shared motivation. This translation emphasizes that Jerusalem's anxiety arose from fear of Herod's reaction—not from hostility toward the Christ. It preserves the narrative logic that Herod's known instability would have stirred political unrest and public fear, not unified opposition to the expected child.

text: He gathered the chief priests and scribes and asked where the Christ was to be born.
“In Bethlehem of Judea,” they replied, “for this is what the prophet wrote:”
“And you, Bethlehem, in Judah, are by no means least among Judah's rulers, for from you will come a ruler to shepherd my people Israel.”

footnotes.content: The image of a shepherd-king unites the royal promise to David (2 Samuel 5:2) with the prophetic hope of Ezekiel 34:23-24; 37:24. It underscores that the Messiah's reign is pastoral—guiding, guarding, and laying down His life for the flock (cf. John 10:11).

text: Herod secretly called the Magi and found out exactly when the star had appeared.
He sent them to Bethlehem and said, “Search carefully for the child. When you find him, tell me so that I too may go and worship him.”
After hearing the king, they went on their way, and the rising star guided them and stopped over where the child was.
When they saw the star, they rejoiced with intense joy!
They entered the house, saw the child with Mary his mother, and bowed down in worship. Then they opened their treasures and presented him with gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh.
Being warned in a dream not to return to Herod, they went home by another route.

After they left, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph, saying, “Rise, take the child and his mother, flee to Egypt, and stay there until I tell you. Herod is searching for the child to destroy him.”
So he rose, took the child and his mother at night, and fled to Egypt.
He stayed there until Herod died, to fulfill what the Lord spoke through the prophet: “Out of Egypt I called my son.”

When Herod realized the Magi had outwitted him, he flew into a rage and ordered the massacre of all boys two and under in Bethlehem and its surrounding region, based on the timing he had learned from them.
To fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah:
“A voice was heard in Ramah, weeping and deep mourning, Rachel weeping for her children. She refused to be comforted, because they are no more.”
After Herod died, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt,
“Rise, take the child and his mother, go into the land of Israel, for those who sought the child's life are now dead.”
He rose, took the child and his mother, and entered Israel.
But when he heard Archelaus ruled Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go. After being warned in a dream, he withdrew to the region of Galilee,
and he settled in a town called Nazareth, to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet: “He will be called a Nazarene.”

title: The Ministry of John the Baptist

text: In those days, John the Baptist appeared, proclaiming in the wilderness of Judea,

footnotes.content: The choice of “proclaiming” over “preaching” reflects more than a stylistic preference—it captures the theological function of John the Baptist's role. The Greek verb kēryssō refers to the act of a herald announcing a royal decree, emphasizing not the messenger's authority but the weight and urgency of the message itself. While “preaching” often suggests personal interpretation or teaching, “proclaiming” more accurately reflects John's identity as a divine forerunner. He was not presenting his own ideas, but announcing what had been decreed from heaven. This is affirmed by his words in John 3:30—“He must increase, but I must decrease”—which encapsulate his role as one who prepares the way and then steps aside for the one who follows.

text: “Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.”

footnotes.content: The Greek word metanoeite literally means “change your mind” or “rethink your direction.” Although the word “repent” is often associated with guilt or emotional sorrow, its biblical meaning is primarily transformational rather than emotional. It denotes a decisive reorientation of one's mind, heart, and behavior toward God. In this translation, “repent” is used not as a term of shame, but as a prophetic command to turn away from self-will and align with divine purpose—just as John the Baptist called his listeners to prepare for the nearness of the kingdom. This preserves the historic language while restoring its original clarity and depth.

text: This is the one spoken of by the prophet Isaiah: “A voice crying out in the wilderness: ‘Prepare the way for the Lord; make his paths straight.’”
John wore a camel-hair garment with a leather belt around his waist, and ate locusts and wild honey.
Jerusalem, all Judea, and the whole Jordan region went out to him,
and they were baptized by him in the Jordan River, confessing their sins.
When he saw many Pharisees and Sadducees coming for baptism, he said, “You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the wrath to come?”
Bear fruit in keeping with repentance.
Do not say to yourselves, ‘We have Abraham as our father,’ for I tell you that God can raise up children for Abraham from these stones.

footnotes.content: When John warns, “Do not presume to say to yourselves, ‘We have Abraham as our father,’” he confronts the belief that ancestry guarantees righteousness. His message is that God's kingdom requires repentance, not heritage. The phrase about raising children from stones underscores God's power to create a true people for himself apart from physical descent.

text: The axe is already at the root of the trees. Every tree that does not bear good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire.
I baptize you with water for repentance, but one who comes after me is mightier than I, and I am not worthy to carry his sandals. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.
His winnowing fork is in his hand. He will clear his threshing floor, gather his wheat into the barn, and burn the chaff with unquenchable fire.
Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John.
But John tried to stop him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, yet you come to me?”
Jesus answered, “Allow it for now, for it is fitting to fulfill all righteousness.” Then John allowed him.
As soon as Jesus rose from the waters of baptism, the heavens opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove coming to rest upon him.
A voice from heaven said, “This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.”
Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil.
After fasting forty days and nights, he was hungry.
The tempter said, “If you are the Son of God, command these stones to become bread.”
Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word from the mouth of God.’”
The devil took him to the holy city and set him on the pinnacle of the temple.

“If you are the Son of God,” he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: ‘He will command His angels concerning you,’ and ‘they will lift you up in their hands, so you will not strike your foot against a stone.’”
Jesus answered, “It is also written: ‘You shall not test the Lord your God.’”
Again the devil took him to a very high mountain, showed him all the world's kingdoms and their splendor.
“All these I will give you if you bow down and worship me,” he said.
Jesus said, “Be gone, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God and only Him you will serve.’”
Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended to him.
When Jesus heard that John had been arrested, he departed to Galilee.
He left Nazareth and settled in Capernaum by the sea, in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali.
To fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah:
“Land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali, way of the sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles.”
“The people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light; on those dwelling in the shadow of death, a light has dawned.”
From then Jesus began to preach: “Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.”
Walking by the Sea of Galilee, he saw two brothers: Simon called Peter, and Andrew, casting a net, for they were fishermen.
“Come, follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.”
At once, they left their nets and followed him.
Going on, he saw James and John in the boat with their father Zebedee, mending nets. He called them.
At once, they left the boat and their father and followed him.
He went through Galilee, teaching in synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness.
His fame spread throughout Syria, and they brought to him all the sick, those with various diseases and torments, the demon-possessed, epileptics, and paralytics, and he healed them.

footnotes.content: “Torments” translates basanismos—bodily pain often linked to demonic affliction. “Demon-oppressed” (not “possessed”) reflects milder cases where control is not total.

text: Large crowds followed him from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan.
Seeing the crowds, Jesus went up the mountain. When he sat down, his disciples came to him.
He opened his mouth and taught them, saying:
Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.

footnotes.content: Greek gē can refer to the land (as in the promised land of Israel) or to the whole earth, depending on context. Jesus draws from Psalm 37 (especially vv. 9, 11, 29), where the meek are promised inheritance of the land as a covenant blessing. In this Beatitude, He reinterprets the promise universally: the meek—those who submit to God—will inherit the earth in the coming kingdom, fulfilling and surpassing Old Testament expectations.

text: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled.

footnotes.content: The word “fulfilled” was chosen over the traditional “filled” to reflect the unmistakably spiritual nature of Jesus' promise. The Greek verb chortazō refers to being fed or satisfied, but in this context, it transcends physical nourishment and speaks to the deep spiritual hunger for righteousness. The hunger and thirst described here are not bodily cravings, but inner longings for God's justice, holiness, and presence to be made real in the soul and in the world. While “filled” might suggest momentary relief, “fulfilled” communicates a lasting, transformative completion. This aligns with the biblical theme of God satisfying the righteous not with food or drink, but with truth, restoration, and divine presence (cf. Isaiah 55:1–2; Psalm 107:9). This rendering captures the eschatological and spiritual intent behind Jesus' words: not temporary comfort, but the ultimate realization of righteousness in the life of the believer and in the kingdom to come.

text: Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy.
Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.
Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God.
Blessed are those persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you, and speak all kinds of evil against you because of me.
Rejoice and be glad, for great is your reward in heaven. For in the same way they persecuted the prophets before you.
You are the salt of the earth. But if the salt loses its preserving power, how can it be restored? It is no longer good for anything, except to be thrown out and trampled underfoot.

footnotes.content: Salt in ancient times was valued primarily for its ability to preserve—not to flavor. Jesus uses this image to reveal the disciple's calling: to slow the world's moral decay and uphold the integrity of God's truth amid corruption. The phrase “loses its preserving power” conveys this responsibility more clearly than “loses its flavor,” which risks reducing the metaphor to mere taste. In Jesus' view, a disciple who no longer preserves truth becomes spiritually useless—cast out, not for lack of passion, but for lack of purpose.

text: You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden.

footnotes.content: The word “shines” was chosen instead of “gives light” to preserve both the poetic weight and the original intent of the metaphor. The Greek verb lampei means “to shine” or “to beam forth.” While “gives light” emphasizes the function of a lamp, it lacks the spiritual resonance intended in Jesus' metaphor. He was not merely describing usefulness, but radiance—one who lives in truth, revealing the divine through their very presence. “Shines” conveys purity, visibility, and the natural outflow of a transformed life. It evokes the image of a soul that illuminates its surroundings not merely by purpose, but by nature.

text: No one lights a lamp and puts it under a basket, but on a stand, and it shines to all in the house.
In the same way, let your light shine before others, so they see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven.
Do not think I came to abolish the Law or the Prophets. I came not to abolish but to fulfill.
Truly I tell you, until heaven and earth pass away, not the smallest letter or stroke of a pen will pass from the Law until all is accomplished.
Whoever breaks the least command and teaches others to do so will be called least in the kingdom of heaven. But whoever obeys and teaches them will be called great in the kingdom of heaven.

footnotes.content: The word “breaks” translates the Greek verb lyō, meaning “to loosen” or “to release.” In legal contexts, it came to mean relaxing or violating a commandment—whether by dismissing its importance or undermining its binding force. Jesus warns not only against defying God's commandments but also against treating even the least of them as negligible, especially when teaching others. The phrase “lives by them” reflects the Greek verb poieō, often rendered “to do” or “to practice.” Here, it implies more than surface-level obedience; it suggests a life genuinely shaped by God's will. The contrast sets a clear standard: greatness in the kingdom is measured not by knowledge, but by wholehearted practice and faithful instruction.

text: Unless your righteousness surpasses that of the scribes and Pharisees, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.

footnotes.content: The scribes were educated experts in the Jewish Law (Torah), responsible for copying, interpreting, and teaching Scripture. They held influential religious roles, often aligned with the Pharisees, and were viewed as the spiritual authorities of their time. However, Jesus frequently challenged their outward piety and legalism, exposing a lack of inward righteousness.

text: You have heard it said to the ancients: “Do not murder,” and “Whoever murders will be liable to judgment.”
But I tell you, whoever is angry with a dear one is liable to judgment. Whoever insults a dear one is liable to the court, and whoever says, ‘You godless one!’ is liable to the fire of Gehenna.

footnotes.content: This translation more faithfully reflects the Greek structure and the rising severity of Jesus' warning. “Worthless” renders the Aramaic raca, a culturally charged insult implying emptiness or contempt. While many versions retain Raca untranslated, we clarify its meaning for modern readers to preserve its original sting. The second insult in the verse, “you godless one,” translates the Greek word moros. This is not a simple attack on intelligence but a spiritually escalated accusation—denoting one who is morally perverse or spiritually rebellious toward God, as echoed in Psalm 14:1: “The fool says in his heart, ‘There is no God.’” In Jesus' context, Gehenna referred to the Valley of Hinnom—a site outside Jerusalem once associated with child sacrifice and divine judgment (cf. Jeremiah 7:31). He invoked it not to threaten literal flames, but to reveal the soul's most tragic fate: to be utterly severed from God's presence. Gehenna came to symbolize the torment of spiritual desolation—the inner wasteland where light is remembered but no longer found, where regret overwhelms, prayers go unanswered, and silence becomes unbearable. It mirrors the soul estranged from its Source, where every “almost” haunts the conscience and longing is sharpened by loss. Yet even such desolation carries a hidden mercy: to feel it is to still be capable of return. This is the destruction Jesus warns of—not just of body, but of relationship with God. And even from there, His mercy reaches out, His voice breaks the silence, offering a path back to the light.

text: If you are offering your gift at the altar and remember that a dear one holds something against you,
leave your gift there, go be reconciled with them, then come offer your gift.

footnotes.content: The phrase “to God” is added at the end of the verse for the sake of modern clarity, even though it does not appear explicitly in the original Greek. In the context of first-century Judaism, presenting a gift at the altar clearly and universally implied an offering to God, and no further specification was needed. However, in modern English—especially for readers unfamiliar with ancient sacrificial practices—the word “offering” may seem vague or transactional. Adding “to God” clarifies the purpose without altering the meaning.

text: Settle quickly with your accuser while you are on the way, or your accuser may hand you to the judge, then to the officer, and you will be thrown into prison.

footnotes.content: The phrase “to court” is added for clarity, though it does not appear explicitly in the Greek. The original expression, using the word hōdō (meaning “on the way”), reads “while you are on the way with them,” but the context—adversary, judge, officer, and prison—clearly implies a legal proceeding. In first-century Jewish culture, it was common for disputes to be resolved privately while traveling to court, often to avoid harsher legal outcomes. This addition helps modern readers recognize that the warning is not about casual disagreement, but about settling a legal or moral conflict before formal judgment is passed. It preserves the original meaning while making the implied legal setting explicit.

text: Truly I tell you, you will not get out until you have paid the last penny.
You have heard it said, “Do not commit adultery.”
But I tell you, anyone who looks at a woman with lust in mind has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
If your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away. It is better to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to be thrown into Gehenna.

footnotes.content: Jesus' command to “pluck out” the eye is not meant literally but reflects a well-known rabbinic metaphor highlighting the radical seriousness of sin. The “eye” symbolizes a gateway of temptation, and the call to remove it dramatizes the need to cut off sin at its root—even if it feels essential or deeply ingrained. This hyperbolic language is meant to awaken the conscience, underscoring that no personal cost is too great if it protects the soul from spiritual ruin. The phrase “thrown into Gehenna (torment of spiritual desolation)” refers to the Greek word geenna, originally linked to the Valley of Hinnom—a site of prophetic judgment and condemnation (cf. Jeremiah 7:31). For a fuller theological and emotional exploration of what Gehenna represents, see the footnote on Matthew 5:22.

text: If your right hand causes you to sin, cut it off and throw it away. It is better to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to be thrown into Gehenna.

footnotes.content: This verse echoes the warning of verse 29, using hyperbolic language to emphasize the urgency of cutting off sin at its source. The Greek verb blēthēnai (“to be thrown”) conveys a decisive and forceful removal—not a gentle departure, but the severity of divine judgment. The phrase “thrown into Gehenna (torment of spiritual desolation)” expresses not physical torment but spiritual exile—the soul's separation from the Source of life. Gehenna represents ultimate spiritual ruin: the place of severed relationship with God. For a fuller theological and emotional exploration of what Gehenna signifies, see the footnote on Matthew 5:22.

text: It was said, “Any man who divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce.”
But I tell you, any man who divorces his wife except for sexual covenant treachery makes her commit adultery. And any man who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.

footnotes.content: The Greek word porneia is often rendered “sexual immorality,” “unchastity,” or “fornication.” However, such translations can sound either vague or culturally disconnected from the biblical context. The phrase “sexual covenant treachery” was chosen to highlight the heart of what porneia implies within the framework of marriage: a willful betrayal of the sacred covenant through sexual unfaithfulness. It captures both the physical act and the spiritual violation, emphasizing not just misconduct, but the relational breach that makes divorce permissible in Jesus' teaching. While more interpretive than “whoredom” or “sexual immorality,” this phrase conveys the theological and emotional significance that best reflects the intent of the original term.

text: You have heard it said to the ancients, “Do not swear falsely, but keep your oaths to the Lord.”
But I tell you do not swear at all. Neither by heaven, for It is God's throne;
nor by the earth, for It is His footstool, or by Jerusalem for It is the city of the great King.

footnotes.content: The term “footstool,” from the Greek hupopodion, refers to a low platform on which a ruler would rest his feet while seated on a throne. In biblical language, it symbolizes dominion and subordination. Jesus echoes Isaiah 66:1, where God declares, “Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool,” affirming his sovereign rule over creation. The phrase “the great King,” from the Greek tou megalou basileōs, is drawn from Psalm 48:2, referring to YHWH, whose presence sanctified Jerusalem as his royal city. By calling it “the city of the great King,” Jesus underscores that even swearing by sacred places is ultimately swearing by God himself, making oath-taking an inescapably divine matter.

text: And do not swear by your head, because you cannot make a single hair white or black.

footnotes.content: Jesus draws attention to something as ordinary as the color of hair to reveal something extraordinary about human limitation. The Greek emphasizes inability—“you are not able” (ou dynasai)—to change even the smallest detail of your own being. In biblical thought, hair often symbolizes identity or divine distinction (cf. Luke 12:7, “even the hairs of your head are all numbered”). By saying one cannot make a single hair white or black, Jesus underscores that we do not even possess full command over our own bodies—let alone the moral or spiritual weight to guarantee truth through an oath. Swearing “by your head,” then, is not only ineffective but presumptuous. The deeper message is that truth does not draw its credibility from human authority, but from alignment with divine reality—where one's word stands simply because it is true.

text: Let your word be ‘Yes’ or ‘No’ for anything more comes from the evil one.

footnotes.content: The phrase “Yes for yes” and “No for no” reflects the intended meaning of the Greek idiom nai nai, ou ou, which emphasizes sincerity and clarity in speech. Rather than prescribing a ritual formula, Jesus is calling for truthfulness that stands without the need for oaths or exaggerated assurances. In first-century usage, the repetition of nai (yes) or ou (no) served to affirm one's words as trustworthy and resolute. This rendering preserves the spirit of Jesus' teaching—that integrity requires no external reinforcement—while helping modern readers recognize that genuine affirmation or denial should be sufficient for a disciple of the kingdom.

text: You have heard it said, ‘Eye for eye, tooth for tooth.’
But I tell you, do not resist an evil person. If anyone slaps you on the right cheek, turn to them the other also.
If anyone sues you for your shirt, give them your coat also.

footnotes.content: Jesus' command to give up even one's cloak goes beyond legal obligation, modeling a radical posture of non-retaliation and voluntary self-sacrifice. This hyperbolic instruction is not about enabling injustice but about exposing it—disarming the oppressor by surrendering what they demand and refusing to enter the power struggle. It teaches that spiritual integrity is more powerful than personal rights.

text: If anyone forces you to go one mile, go with them two.
Give to whoever asks, and do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow.
You have heard it said, ‘Love your neighbor and hate your enemy.’

footnotes.content: The command to “love your neighbor” comes directly from Leviticus 19:18 and is rightly attributed to the Law. However, the phrase “and will hate your enemy” does not appear in the Old Testament. It reflects a common interpretive tradition in Second Temple Judaism, likely influenced by Israel's historical posture toward national enemies and reinforced by certain imprecatory psalms. Some sectarian groups, such as the Qumran community (Dead Sea Scrolls), explicitly taught to “love the sons of light and hate the sons of darkness.” Jesus challenges this distortion by expanding the command of love beyond tribal, ethnic, and religious boundaries. His correction restores the true spirit of the Law, revealing that love is not reserved for allies but is to be extended even to enemies—those who oppose, injure, or persecute us.

text: But I tell you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who persecute you.
So you may be children of your Father in heaven. He makes his sun rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous.
If you love those who love you, what reward will you have? Do not even tax-collectors do the same?

footnotes.content: In first-century Jewish society, tax collectors were widely despised as collaborators with the Roman occupiers and were often associated with corruption and extortion. Jesus invokes them here not to condone their behavior, but to make a sharp contrast: even those considered morally compromised love those who love them. By comparing this to mere reciprocal affection, Jesus reveals that kingdom love must exceed common social behavior—it must be rooted in grace, not merit.

text: If you greet only your own, what are you doing more than others? Do not even the Gentiles do the same?

footnotes.content: The term “Gentiles” translates the Greek ethnē, referring to non-Jewish peoples. In this context, Jesus uses it not to demean other nations, but to emphasize that even those outside God's covenant with Israel naturally love those who love them. His point is that true righteousness goes beyond cultural norms and extends love even to enemies—mirroring the character of God Himself.

text: Be perfect therefore, as your Father in heaven is perfect.
Beware of practicing your righteousness to impress others, for then you will have no reward from your Father in heaven.
When you give, do not broadcast publicly or in synagogues like hypocrites seeking praise from people. Truly I tell you, they have received their reward.

footnotes.content: The phrase “sound a trumpet” is metaphorical, referring to showy, attention-seeking behavior. There is no evidence that literal trumpets were used in acts of charity. Jesus is condemning those who turn giving into public performance to gain praise. The phrase “they have their reward in full” translates the Greek apechousin ton misthon autōn; the verb apechō was used in financial contexts to indicate full payment received—meaning public praise is the only reward they will get, with nothing further from God.

text: But when you give, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing.
Then your giving will be secret, and your Father who sees what is done in secret will repay you openly.
When you pray, do not perform in public like hypocrites who love to be seen. Truly I tell you, they have received their reward.
But when you pray, go into your room, shut the door, and pray in secret to your Father, for He who sees what is done in secret will reward you openly.

footnotes.content: The Greek phrase ho blepōn en tō kryptō means “the one who sees in secret.” The words “what is done” were added for clarity, making the implied action explicit in the phrase “who sees what is done in secret.”

text: When you pray, do not repeat mindlessly like pagans who think they will be heard because of their many words.

footnotes.content: The phrase “meaningless repetition” translates the Greek verb battalogēsēte, a rare term possibly derived from the onomatopoeic root battal-, suggesting stammering or babbling. While the precise etymology is uncertain, the meaning in context is clear: speaking with excessive, empty, or mechanical repetition. Jesus is not condemning repeated prayer—as seen in the Psalms or in his own prayers—but is warning against ritualistic or performance-driven speech, such as was common in pagan practices where word quantity was prioritized over heart sincerity. The expression “meaningless repetitions” captures this distinction with clarity for modern readers, avoiding confusion with genuine, heartfelt persistence in prayer.

text: Do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you ask.
“Pray like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name.
Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven.
Give us this day our daily bread.
Forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.
And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. For yours is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory forever, Amen.”

footnotes.content: The concluding phrase, “For Yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever. Amen,” does not appear in the earliest Greek manuscripts of Matthew but is included in many later Byzantine texts and became part of the church's liturgical tradition. It was likely added during public worship and later incorporated into manuscript copies. While it may not have been spoken by Jesus in this specific context, the doxology is theologically consistent with the message of the prayer and serves as a fitting closure of praise. It is retained here for its deep devotional impact and its long-standing use in Christian worship, in keeping with this translation's emphasis on spiritual resonance and reader edification.

text: If you forgive people's trespasses, your Father in heaven will forgive you.
But if you do not forgive others, your Father in heaven will also not forgive you.
When you fast, do not look gloomy like the hypocrites, for they disfigure their faces to be seen fasting by people. Truly I tell you, they have received their reward.
But when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face,
so your fasting may not be seen by people but by your Father in secret; and your Father who sees what is done in secret will reward you openly.
Do not pile up treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal.

footnotes.content: “Pile up” renders thēsaurizō here negatively (greedy hoarding). It contrasts with the positive “store up … in heaven” in v. 20.

text: But store up treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust destroys, and where thieves do not break in or steal.
For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eye is pure, your whole body will be full of light.

footnotes.content: The Greek word haplous refers to an eye that is single-minded, sincere, and morally undivided. It implies integrity of purpose—a clear, generous, and undistracted vision. This is not about physical health, but about ethical clarity: seeing the world through a heart free from greed, lust, or double motives. Jesus contrasts this with the “evil eye” in the next verse, a Hebrew idiom for selfishness or envy (cf. Proverbs 28:22). A “pure” eye lets in the light of truth; a corrupt eye darkens the whole self.

text: But if your eye is evil, your whole body will be full of darkness. If then the light you rely on is darkness, how deep is that darkness.

footnotes.content: The word “evil” translates the Greek ponēros, which refers to a morally and spiritually corrupt condition. Jesus is not merely speaking of sinful behavior, but of a distorted spiritual perception—when the “eye,” representing one's inner moral vision, is diseased. The result is that the entire person becomes darkened. The phrase “if the light you rely on is darkness” heightens the danger: it refers to those who believe they are enlightened, yet are profoundly self-deceived. The concluding cry, “how deep is that darkness,” is a Hebraic idiom expressing overwhelming moral blindness. It echoes Isaiah 5:20, where good is called evil and light is called darkness, underscoring the terrifying depth of spiritual delusion.

text: No one can serve two masters. Either you will hate one and love the other, or be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and Mammon.

footnotes.content: Wealth here translates the Greek word mamōnas (transliterated as Mammon), originally from Aramaic. It refers not merely to money or possessions but to wealth as a spiritual power—personified as a rival master to God. Jesus uses Mammon to expose the heart's divided loyalties, showing that true devotion cannot be shared between God and materialism.

text: Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat or drink, or about your body, and what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothing?

footnotes.content: Jesus is not condemning planning or responsibility, but warning against anxious preoccupation with material needs. This verse follows his teaching that one cannot serve both God and wealth—the Greek word mamōnas, often personified to represent wealth as a spiritual rival. Worry, in this context, becomes a competing master—dividing the heart and pulling it away from trust in divine provision. By asking, “Is not life more than food…?” Jesus redirects attention to the God who gave life, affirming that the One who created the body can be trusted to sustain it. The command not to worry is not about passive detachment, but about active trust in a Father who knows and provides.

text: Look at the birds of the air. They do not sow, or reap, or gather into barns, yet your Father in heaven feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they?
Who among you by worrying can add a single hour to your life?
And why do you worry about clothing? Consider how the lilies of the field grow; they do not labor or spin,
yet I tell you, not even Solomon in all his splendor was clothed like one of these.
If God so clothes the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow is thrown into the oven, will He not much more clothe you? You of little faith?
Therefore, do not worry, saying, “What will we eat? or drink? or wear?”
For the Gentiles chase these things, and your Father in heaven knows you need them.
Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.

footnotes.content: "His righteousness" refers to God's moral standard, justice, and covenant faithfulness. To seek His righteousness means to pursue a life aligned with God's will—both in outward actions and inward devotion—reflecting what is right and pleasing to Him.

text: Therefore, do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will carry its own burdens. Each day has enough trouble of its own.

footnotes.content: Jesus teaches a posture of present-focused trust. “Tomorrow will carry its own burdens” means that future troubles will arrive in their time, and worrying now only adds unnecessary weight. Each day brings its own challenges; faith calls us to live one day at a time, trusting God for what lies ahead.

text: Judge not, or you too will be judged.

footnotes.content: “Judge not” does not prohibit all moral or doctrinal evaluation. It forbids harsh, hypocritical judgment. The Greek verb krinō means to distinguish or pronounce judgment. In context (vv. 2–5), Jesus commands self-examination before criticizing others. This aligns with His teaching elsewhere (cf. John 7:24), where He permits righteous judgment rooted in humility and truth.

text: For with the measure you use, it will be measured back to you.
Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye, but miss the log in your own?
How can you say to your sister, ‘Let me remove the speck from your eye,’ while a log is still in yours?
You hypocrite! First remove the log from your own eye, then you'll see clearly to take the speck from a fellow believer's eye.
Do not give what is holy to dogs, or throw your pearls before swine, lest they trample them underfoot, turn and tear you to pieces.

footnotes.content: The phrase “turn on you and tear you to pieces” comes from the Greek verbs straphentes (“to turn”) and rhēxōsin (“to tear apart”), painting a scene of sudden aggression. After rejecting what is holy, the pigs lash out at the giver. Jesus warns that offering sacred truth to those hostile or unreceptive may provoke backlash. The imagery of dogs and pigs—symbols of impurity in Jewish culture—underscores the need for discernment, not elitism, in sharing what is holy.

text: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you.
For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks, it will be opened.
Who among you, if his son asks for bread, would give him a stone?
Or if his daughter asks for a fish, would hand her a serpent?
If you, being evil, yet know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask Him?
Whatever you want others to do for you, do the same for them. This is foundational in the Law and the Prophets.

footnotes.content: The phrase “is foundational in the Law and the Prophets” reflects the Greek wording houtos gar estin ho nomos kai hoi prophētai, which literally means, “for this is the Law and the Prophets.” While some translations render this as “sums up,” such phrasing could mislead—since in Matthew 22:40, Jesus names love of God and neighbor as the two great commandments upon which all the Law and the Prophets depend. The Golden Rule captures the ethical essence of how we treat others, but not the full relational scope of Jesus' teaching. The phrase “foundational in” was chosen because it affirms the deep significance of this principle within the Hebrew Scriptures, while avoiding the implication that it alone captures the entirety of the Law's purpose. It reflects a core moral pillar without overstating its exclusivity.

text: Enter through the narrow gate, for the gate is wide and the road is broad that leads to destruction, and many enter through it.
For the gate is narrow and the road is hard that leads to life, and those who find it are few.
Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves.
By their fruit, you will recognize them. Are grapes gathered from thorns, or figs from thistles?
Every good tree bears good fruit, but an evil tree bears bad fruit.
A good tree cannot bear evil fruit, and an evil tree cannot bear good fruit.
Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.
It follows, by their fruit you will recognize them.
Not everyone who says to me, “Lord, Lord,” will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father in heaven.
Many will say to me on that day, “Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, cast out demons in your name, and perform many mighty works in your name?”
Then I will tell them plainly, “I never knew you. Depart from me, you workers of lawlessness.”

footnotes.content: Jesus' words here express more than denial—they expose a relational void. “I never knew you” does not mean God lacked information about them, but that there was no intimacy, no communion of spirit. These were people who claimed His name but lived in contradiction to His will. To be called a “worker of lawlessness” is not just about sinful acts—it means living in persistent rebellion, outside the order and heart of God's kingdom. This is not rejection of sincere strugglers, but of those who practiced spiritual performance without transformation. The command to “depart” is the sorrowful justice of a Savior whose invitation was ignored until it was too late.

text: Therefore, everyone who hears these words and lives by them is like a wise person who built their house on the rock.
The rain fell, the rivers rose, the winds blew and beat against that house, yet it did not fall, for it had been founded on the rock.
But everyone who hears these words and does not live by them is like a foolish person who built their house on the sand.
The rain fell, the rivers rose, the winds blew and struck that house, and it fell, and mighty was its fall.

footnotes.content: This verse portrays the end of a life built without obedience to Jesus' teaching. The fall of the house represents the spiritual ruin that comes from hearing His word but not living by it. The contrast throughout the parable makes clear: only those who build on the foundation of His word—by doing it—will withstand the storms of judgment and life.

text: When Jesus finished speaking, the crowds were astonished at His teaching.
For he taught with authority, not like their scribes.

footnotes.content: The word exousia refers to intrinsic authority—not merely permission or delegated power, but divine right. Jesus' authority sharply contrasted with that of the scribes, who taught by citing rabbinic tradition. While they relied on precedent, He spoke with the weight of original, divine truth—as one who wrote the Law, not merely interpreted it. The crowd's astonishment was not merely intellectual but spiritual. They sensed the difference between inherited commentary and living revelation. Even His enemies later confessed, “No one ever spoke like this man” (John 7:46).

text: When Jesus came down the mountain, great crowds followed him.
Suddenly, a leper came and knelt before him. “Lord, if you will, you can make me clean.”
Jesus reached out, touched him, and said, “I will; be cleansed.” Immediately his leprosy was gone.
Then Jesus said, “Tell no one, but go show yourself to the priest and offer the gift Moses commanded, as a testimony to them.”

footnotes.content: Jesus' command, “See that you tell no one,” reflects what scholars call the Messianic Secret—a recurring pattern in the Gospels where Jesus intentionally downplays public recognition of his miracles. This wasn't due to timidity, but strategic timing: premature fame could distort his mission, incite political unrest, or provoke opposition before the appointed hour. The instruction to show himself to the priest aligns with Leviticus 14, fulfilling the law and offering the healing as testimony—not spectacle. Jesus honors both covenant protocol and divine discretion.

text: When Jesus entered Capernaum, a centurion came and pleaded with him.
“Lord, my servant lies at home, paralyzed and in terrible pain.”
Jesus said, “I will come and heal him.”
But the centurion replied, “Lord, I am not worthy to have you under my roof. Just say the word, and my servant will be healed.”
For I too am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I say to one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; ‘Come,’ and he comes; ‘Do this,’ and he does it.
Jesus was amazed and said to those following, “Truly I tell you, I have not found such great faith in all Israel.”
Many will come from east and west to feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven.
But the sons of the kingdom will be banished to the outer darkness, where there is weeping and gnashing of teeth.

footnotes.content: The phrase “sons of the kingdom” refers to those—especially within Israel—who assumed their place in God's kingdom was guaranteed by ancestry or covenant identity, yet rejected Jesus as the Christ (cf. Matthew 21:43). The word “banished” translates the Greek ekballō, meaning “to throw out,” here signifying a solemn and final exclusion from God's presence. “Outer darkness” evokes a state of spiritual exile, cut off from the joy and light of the kingdom. The weeping and gnashing of teeth, repeated elsewhere (e.g. Matthew 13:42; 22:13), express profound regret and anguish. Jesus reverses expectations: those once assumed to be far off are welcomed in, while many presumed insiders are shut out—by their own unbelief, not divine injustice.

text: Then Jesus said to the centurion, “Go. As you believed, so let it be done for you.” And his servant was healed that very hour.
Jesus entered Peter's house and saw his mother-in-law sick in bed with a fever.
He touched her hand, and the fever left her. She rose and began to serve them.
That evening they brought him many who were demon-oppressed. He cast out the spirits with a word and healed all the sick.
To fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah: “He took our sicknesses and bore our diseases.”
When Jesus saw a great crowd around him, he gave orders to cross to the other side.
A scribe came and said, “Teacher, I will follow you wherever you go.”
Jesus replied, “Foxes have dens, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.”

footnotes.content: The phrase ‘Son of Man’ is a messianic title rooted in Daniel 7:13, where one like a son of man is given authority by God. Jesus uses this title to reveal both His humanity and divine appointment. His saying contrasts the comfort of worldly homes with His own path of rejection and pilgrimage, emphasizing the cost of true discipleship.

text: Another disciple said, “Lord, let me first go and bury my father.”
But Jesus said, “Follow me, and let the dead bury their own dead.”

footnotes.content: Jesus' command, “Let the dead bury their own dead,” is not a rejection of love or grief, but a call to radical spiritual urgency. The first “dead” refers to the spiritually unresponsive; the second, to the physically deceased. Though burial was a sacred duty in Jewish tradition, Jesus reveals that even the most honorable obligations must not delay obedience. His words expose the danger of postponing discipleship—even for reasons that seem noble.

text: When Jesus got into the boat, his disciples followed him.
Suddenly, a violent storm arose on the sea, and the boat was being swamped by the waves. But Jesus was asleep.
The disciples came and woke him, saying, “Lord, save us! We are perishing!”
He said to them, “Why are you afraid, you of little faith?” Then he got up and rebuked the winds and the sea, and there was a great calm.
The men were amazed and said, “What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey him!”
When he came to the other side, to the region of the Gadarenes, two demon-possessed men met him, coming out of the tombs. They were so violent that no one could pass that way.
Suddenly, they cried out, “What do you want with us, Jesus, Son of God? Have you come to torment us before the appointed time?”
A large herd of pigs was feeding some distance away.
The demons begged him, “If you cast us out, allow us to go into the herd of pigs.”
He said to them, “Go.” So they came out and went into the pigs, and the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned.
Those tending them fled and went into the city and reported everything, including what happened to the demon-possessed men.
Suddenly, the whole city came out to meet Jesus. When they saw him, they begged him to leave their region.

footnotes.content: The townspeople's request for Jesus to leave may seem surprising after witnessing a miraculous deliverance, but it reflects the tension between divine power and human fear. In casting out the demons, Jesus disrupted their economy—the loss of the pigs likely represented a major financial blow. But more than material loss, his presence exposed spiritual realities they were not prepared to face. Like in Luke 5:8, where Peter falls at Jesus' feet and says, “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man,” this moment reveals how holiness can provoke discomfort, especially when it confronts hidden darkness. Rather than rejoice over the liberation of the men, the town recoils at the cost and the presence of one whose authority they could not control.

text: Jesus stepped into a boat, crossed over, and came to his town.
Suddenly, some men brought him a paralyzed man on a mat. Seeing their faith, Jesus said, “Take heart, child; your sins are forgiven.”
Some scribes said to themselves, “He is blaspheming.”
Jesus knew their thoughts and said, “Why think evil in your hearts?”
Which is easier to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Rise and walk’?
But so you know the Son of Man has authority to forgive sins, he said to the paralyzed man, “Rise, pick up your mat and go home.”
He rose and went home.
When the crowd saw this, they were awestruck and praised God who had given such authority to humankind.
As Jesus passed by, he saw a man named Matthew at the tax booth. “Follow me,” he said. And Matthew got up and followed him.
While Jesus was eating at the house, many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with him and his disciples.
When the Pharisees saw this, they said to his disciples, “Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?”
Jesus heard and said, “It is not the healthy who need a physician, but the sick.”
Go and learn what this means: ‘I desire mercy, not sacrifice.’ For I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.

footnotes.content: When Jesus quotes, “I DESIRE MERCY AND NOT SACRIFICE,” He draws from Hosea 6:6 to confront the Pharisees' distorted understanding of righteousness. In its original context, this verse expresses YHWH's longing for covenantal loyalty—steadfast love expressed through compassionate action—rather than mere ritual compliance. Jesus applies it here to expose the emptiness of external religiosity that lacks mercy toward sinners. His mission is not to reward the self-righteous, but to call those aware of their need. The quotation reaffirms that God values a heart aligned with His mercy more than outward displays of piety, and it restores the prophetic call to love over legalism. The directive, “Go and learn what this means,” was a rabbinic challenge—an invitation to true understanding, not mere familiarity with Scripture.

title: The Old Cannot Bear the New

text: Then John's disciples came and asked, “Why do we and the Pharisees fast often, while your disciples do not?”
Jesus said, “Can wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them? Days will come when he is taken away from them; then they will fast.”

footnotes.content: The “bridegroom” metaphor portrays Jesus as the long-awaited Christ and covenant redeemer, drawing on Old Testament imagery where God is depicted as Israel's husband (cf. Isaiah 54:5; Hosea 2:16–20). His presence marks a time of joy and celebration, not mourning. Just as wedding guests do not fast while the bridegroom is present, so Jesus' disciples rejoice in his presence. But the phrase “the bridegroom will be taken away” foreshadows his death—when sorrow and fasting will be appropriate. The metaphor emphasizes the newness, intimacy, and temporary nature of Jesus' earthly ministry.

text: No one puts a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment, for it tears away and makes the rip worse.

footnotes.content: The patch metaphor emphasizes the futility of combining Jesus' new way with the old system of religious observance. An unshrunk patch sewn onto an old garment will pull away when washed, worsening the tear. Likewise, trying to attach Jesus' message of renewal to the brittle framework of legalism results in damage, not repair. The metaphor warns that the kingdom of God cannot be treated as a supplement to old covenant rituals—it demands a complete reweaving of the fabric of spiritual life.

text: No one pours new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the skins burst, the wine spills, and both are lost. New wine is poured into fresh wineskins, and both are preserved.

footnotes.content: The image of new wine in old wineskins illustrates the incompatibility between Jesus' kingdom message and the old religious structures. New wine, still fermenting, would burst brittle old skins—just as the dynamic, Spirit-filled life of the kingdom cannot be contained by rigid traditions or legalistic systems. The “fresh wineskins” represent a renewed vessel—new hearts, new covenant, new community—capable of bearing the transformative power of Jesus' teaching without rupture. The metaphor affirms that Jesus did not come to patch up old religion, but to inaugurate something entirely new.

text: While he was speaking, a synagogue official came and knelt before him. “My daughter has just died,” he said. “But come, lay your hand on her, and she will live.”
Jesus got up and followed him, and so did his disciples.
Suddenly, a woman who had suffered bleeding for twelve years came up behind him and touched the edge of his cloak.
For she said to herself, “If I only touch his cloak, I will be healed.”
Jesus turned, saw her, and said, “Take heart, daughter. Your faith has saved you.” And the woman was healed at that very moment.
When Jesus entered the official's house, he saw the flute players and the uproar of mourning.
He said to them, “Leave, for the girl is not dead but asleep.” And they laughed at him.
When the crowd was sent out, Jesus went in, took her by the hand, and the girl arose.
News of this spread throughout all that region.
As Jesus went on from there, two blind men followed him, crying out, “Have mercy on us, Son of David!”

footnotes.content: The title “Son of David” is a messianic designation drawn from God's covenant with David in 2 Samuel 7:12–16, where He promises that David's throne will be established forever. By the time of Jesus, this phrase had become a widespread hope for a coming deliverer—a kingly figure who would restore Israel. When the blind men cry out, “Son of David,” they are not merely recognizing Jesus' lineage, but proclaiming Him as the long-awaited Messiah. Their plea reveals both theological insight and personal desperation: though physically blind, they perceive Jesus more clearly than many who see. This moment highlights how faith often resides not in physical sight, but in spiritual recognition of who Jesus truly is.

text: When he entered the house, the blind men came to him. Jesus said, “Do you believe I am able to do this?” They said, “Yes, Lord.”
Then he touched their eyes and said, “According to your faith, let it be done to you.”
Their eyes were opened, and Jesus sternly warned them, “See that no one knows.”
Yet they went out and spread the news about him throughout all that region.
As they were leaving, a mute demon-possessed man was brought to Jesus.
When the demon was cast out, the mute man spoke. The crowds were amazed and said, “Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel!”
But the Pharisees said, “He casts out demons by the ruler of demons!”
Jesus went through all the towns and villages, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of disease and affliction.
When he saw the crowds, he ached with compassion for them, because they were weary and scattered, like sheep without a shepherd.

footnotes.content: The Greek verb splagchnizomai, translated here as “moved with compassion,” expresses a deep, visceral mercy—divine sorrow that moves toward healing. The people are described as “dejected and helpless,” reflecting the Greek eskylmenoi (worn down, mistreated) and erimmenoi (abandoned). Many translations use “harassed and helpless” (ESV, NIV), which underplays the spiritual grief Jesus saw. The phrase “like sheep without a shepherd” echoes Numbers 27:17 and Ezekiel 34:5–16, where God laments failed leadership and promises to shepherd his people. Jesus' compassion here fulfills that promise.

text: Then he said to his disciples, “The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few.”
Therefore pray to the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his harvest.

footnotes.content: The phrase “pray earnestly” translates the Greek verb deomai, meaning to plead or implore—especially in prayer to God. The “harvest” symbolizes a world ready for the kingdom, yet lacking faithful workers. Jesus invites his disciples to intercede, not because God is reluctant, but to align with His mission. The Lord of the harvest sends whom He chooses—and often, those who pray become the ones He sends (cf. 10:1).

text: Jesus called his twelve disciples and gave them authority to drive out unclean spirits and heal every disease and affliction.
These are the names of the twelve apostles: first, Simon (called Peter), and Andrew his brother; James son of Zebedee, and John his brother;
Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus;
Simon the Zealot; and Judas Iscariot, the one who later betrayed him.
Jesus sent out these twelve, commanding them: “Do not go among the Gentiles or enter any Samaritan town;
go instead to the lost sheep of Israel.
As you go, proclaim: ‘The kingdom of heaven has come near.’
Heal the sick, cleanse lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received; freely give.
Do not take gold, silver, or copper for your belts;
no bag for the journey, no extra tunic, no sandals, no staff; for the worker is worthy of his provisions.”

footnotes.content: “Provisions” refers to the basic needs of those doing God's work—such as food, shelter, and clothing. Jesus affirms that those who serve the kingdom should be supported by those they minister to.

text: “When you enter any town or village, find out who is worthy and stay there until you leave.
As you enter the house, greet it.
If the house is worthy, let your peace come upon it; but if it is not, let your peace return to you.
If anyone does not welcome you or listen to your words, shake the dust off your feet as you leave that house or town.

footnotes.content: The gesture of shaking dust from one's feet was a symbolic act of renouncing responsibility and fellowship with that place. In Jewish custom, it was sometimes performed when leaving Gentile territory, expressing a break in spiritual solidarity. Here, it underscores the severity of rejecting the kingdom message. It marks that town as covenantally excluded.

text: Truly I tell you, it will be more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on judgment day than for that town.”

footnotes.content: This comparative phrase expresses a severe divine judgment using the infamous fate of Sodom and Gomorrah as a benchmark. In context, it heightens the spiritual accountability of those who reject the message brought by Jesus' messengers. It also implies that judgment is graduated, not uniform. This is a key point in Jesus' theology of divine justice.

text: “Listen, I am sending you out like sheep among wolves; so be wise as serpents and innocent as doves.
Watch out for people. They will deliver you over to courts and flog you in their synagogues.
You will be brought before governors and kings because of me, as a testimony to them and to the nations.
Do not worry about how or what you should say; it will be given to you at that moment.
You will not be the one speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you.
A family member will betray another to death, and a father his child; children will rise against their parents and have them killed.
You will be hated by everyone because of my name, but the one who endures to the end will be saved.
When they persecute you in one town, flee to another. Truly I tell you, you will not finish going through the towns of Israel before the Son of Man comes.”

footnotes.content: This verse contains one of the most debated sayings of Jesus. The phrase “before the Son of Man comes” can be understood in several ways: as a reference to (1) Jesus' resurrection and post-resurrection appearances, (2) His coming in judgment against Jerusalem in 70 AD, or (3) His final return at the end of the age. The immediate context favors a near-term fulfillment—Jesus comforting His disciples during a time of Jewish mission and persecution. The phrase may reflect a recurring pattern in prophetic speech where one event (e.g., judgment on Israel) foreshadows a greater one (the final coming). Rather than specifying chronology, Jesus assures His disciples that their mission will not be in vain and that He will come in power before their work is exhausted.

text: “A disciple is not greater than their teacher, nor a slave greater than their master.
It is enough for a disciple to strive to become like his teacher, and a slave to be like his master. If they called the head of the house ‘Beelzebul,’ how much more the members of his household?

footnotes.content: When Jesus says, “If they have called the master of the house Beelzebul,” he refers to accusations that he worked by demonic power (see Matthew 12:24). The term “Beelzebul” likely mocks Baal as “lord of dung.” His point is that disciples should not expect better treatment than their master. If Jesus himself was slandered, his followers will face even worse. The phrase “members of his household” emphasizes spiritual kinship—and shared suffering.

text: Therefore do not fear them. Nothing is hidden that will not be revealed, or a secret that will not be known.
What I tell you in the dark, speak in the light; what you hear whispered, proclaim from the rooftops.
Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather, fear Him who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.
Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them falls to the ground apart from your Father.
Even the hairs of your head are all numbered.

footnotes.content: The statement “Even the hairs of your head are all numbered” uses hyperbole to express God's intimate care. In Jewish idiom, counting one's hairs meant absolute attentiveness—nothing escapes his knowledge. Jesus assures that even what seems trivial is known and valued by the Father.

text: Therefore, do not fear. You are worth more than many sparrows.”
“Whoever acknowledges me before others, I will also acknowledge before my Father in heaven.
But whoever denies me before others, I will also deny before my Father in heaven.”
“Do not think I came to bring peace to the earth. I did not come to bring peace, but a sword.

footnotes.content: Jesus' statement is not a call to violence but a warning about the unavoidable division caused by the truth of the Gospel. The “sword” represents the piercing nature of God's Word and the Truth it reveals—dividing light from darkness, loyalty from rebellion, and even kin from kin. The Greek word machaira refers to a small sword or dagger, and in biblical usage, it often symbolizes judgment or spiritual separation (cf. Hebrews 4:12). Jesus is declaring that allegiance to Him will expose and fracture even the closest earthly relationships, just as prophesied in Micah 7:6. This sword is the dividing force of Truth—not violence, but conviction.

text: I came to divide a man against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.

footnotes.content: The phrase “I have come to divide a man against his father…” echoes Micah 7:6, where the breakdown of family trust is described as a symptom of societal collapse. Jesus is not promoting conflict but revealing that allegiance to him often provokes division, even within the most sacred earthly bonds. Following him demands a higher loyalty than cultural or familial expectations. His coming exposes hearts and forces a choice—creating division not by aggression, but by truth.

text: A person's enemies will be members of their own household.
Anyone who loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me; and anyone who loves son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.
Whoever does not take up their cross and follow me is not worthy of me.
Whoever finds their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for my sake will find it.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “Whoever finds their life will lose it…” expresses the paradox at the heart of discipleship. To “find your life” means to cling to ego—your self-centered identity, your control, your ambitions. But in grasping for self, you forfeit what matters most: life rooted in God. Conversely, “losing your life for my sake” is not merely about martyrdom—it's the daily surrender of ego, the laying down of personal sovereignty to receive divine purpose. In dying to the false self, you awaken to the true life God offers. Jesus invites us to trade the fragile illusion of control for the indestructible freedom of trust.

text: “Whoever receives you receives me, and whoever receives me receives Him who sent me.
