Whoever receives a prophet because they are a prophet will receive a prophet's reward, and whoever receives a righteous person because they are righteous will receive a righteous person's reward.

footnotes.content: The phrase “will receive a prophet's reward” refers to the shared blessing that comes from honoring a prophet's mission. In Jewish tradition, a prophet was God's mouthpiece, and to receive one—meaning to support, host, or heed their message—was to participate in their sacred work. Jesus affirms that even those who aren't prophets themselves but honor one because they recognize their role will share in their spiritual reward. The same applies to “a righteous person”—this speaks to how God notices not only those on the front lines, but also those who stand beside them in faith and solidarity.

text: And whoever gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because they are my disciple, truly I tell you, will never lose their reward.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “a cup of cold water to one of these little ones” highlights how even the smallest act of kindness toward a disciple—motivated by their identity in Christ—is seen and rewarded by God. “Little ones” refers not to children, but to seemingly insignificant followers. Jesus affirms that no act of love done for his sake is ever overlooked.

text: When Jesus finished instructing his twelve, he moved on to teach and preach in their towns.
But John, hearing in prison about the works of Christ, sent two of his disciples
to ask him, “Are you the one to come, or should we expect another?”

footnotes.content: The question “Are you the one who is to come?” reveals John's uncertainty, likely provoked by his imprisonment and unmet expectations. The Greek phrase uses ho erchomenos (“the Coming One”), a messianic title derived from passages like Psalm 118:26 and Daniel 7:13. This title signals hope for divine deliverance—but John's use here implies struggle, not certainty. His honesty reflects the spiritual tension between expectation and reality.

text: Jesus replied, “Go tell John what you hear and see:
the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor are preached the good news.”
‘Blessed is the one who does not turn away from me.’

footnotes.content: The word translated as “turn away” comes from the Greek verb skandalizō, often rendered “stumble” or “fall away.” However, in modern English, “stumble” has become too soft, and “fall away” risks misinterpretation—as if Jesus were the cause of someone's sin or apostasy. In reality, skandalizō points to the offense or spiritual resistance people feel when confronted with Christ's humility, mercy, or unexpected ways. The rendering “turn away because of me” was chosen to preserve this tension: it describes the heart that recoils from Jesus when He does not meet worldly expectations. This becomes especially clear in context—John the Baptist, imprisoned and doubting, sends messengers to ask if Jesus is truly the One. Rather than rebuke, Jesus responds with blessing: the one who remains faithful, even when God's work unfolds in paradox, is blessed. This translation honors the original meaning while making it spiritually and emotionally accessible to the reader.

text: As they departed, Jesus spoke to the crowds about John: ‘What did you go out to the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?’

footnotes.content: The image of a “reed shaken by the wind” evokes instability, cowardice, or conformism—qualities opposite to John the Baptist's prophetic character. In first-century Palestine, reeds were common along the Jordan and easily bent by the breeze. Jesus uses the metaphor rhetorically to ask whether the people expected someone fickle or afraid. The implied answer is no: John was unwavering, even unto imprisonment. The phrase exposes the contrast between public fascination and actual recognition of prophetic truth. By raising this question, Jesus affirms John's moral fortitude and prophetic authority.

text: ‘Or a man in soft clothing? Behold, those who wear fine garments live in kings' palaces.’
‘But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, and more than a prophet.’
‘Behold, I send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you.’
‘Truly I tell you, among those born of women, none has risen greater than John the Baptist, yet the least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he.’

footnotes.content: Jesus declares John the Baptist to be the greatest of those “born of women”—a Hebrew idiom meaning all human beings. John's greatness lies in his role as the final prophet of the old covenant, the one who directly announced and identified the Christ. Yet paradoxically, the “least in the kingdom of heaven” is said to be greater—not in holiness or courage, but in spiritual position. John stood on the threshold of the new covenant but did not live to witness its fulfillment. Those who enter the kingdom after Christ's resurrection partake in a deeper spiritual reality—sealed by the Spirit, covered by the cross, and joined to the risen life of the Christ. It is a shift in redemptive history: greatness is no longer measured by proximity to prophecy, but by participation in the kingdom Christ has inaugurated.

text: ‘From the days of John the Baptist until now, the kingdom of heaven has been advancing forcefully, and the zealous take hold of it.’

footnotes.content: The Greek verbs biazetai (“is forcefully advancing”) and harpazousin (“are laying hold of it”) paint a picture of intensity and movement. The phrase “the zealous are laying hold of it” was chosen to express not just effort, but the emotional desperation of those who know they cannot afford to miss the kingdom. Jesus is not describing violent aggressors but spiritually awakened people—those whose longing for salvation pushes them past fear, pride, and delay. “Zealous” captures the soul-deep resolve of the poor in spirit, the broken, the hungry, who rush toward the kingdom as their only hope. This interpretation aligns with Luke 16:16, where Jesus says, “everyone is forcing their way into it”—not by violence, but by the urgency of awakened faith. Grammatically, biazetai may be understood either as a middle-voice verb (“the kingdom is forcefully advancing”) or as a passive (“is being attacked”), but in context, the middle fits best. The language points to the disruptive inbreaking of God's reign, met with passionate response by those who recognize its worth.

text: ‘For all the prophets and the law prophesied until John.’
‘And if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who was to come.’

footnotes.content: When Jesus says, “he is Elijah,” he affirms that John the Baptist fulfills the long-awaited role prophesied in Malachi 4:5. The phrase “if you are willing to accept it” reveals that this identity is spiritually perceived rather than universally obvious. The crowd expected Elijah to return in fire, yet he came in camel's hair, calling for repentance. Jesus leaves room for mystery—John may not look like the Elijah of old, but he bears the mantle, the mission, and the spirit of the forerunner. This subtle ambiguity invites the listener to decide whether they are willing to see prophetic fulfillment not by appearance, but by function and divine appointment.

text: ‘Whoever has ears, let them listen.’
‘To what shall I compare this generation? It is like children sitting in the marketplace, calling to their companions,’
‘We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn.’

footnotes.content: The saying, “We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn,” draws from a children's game mimicking weddings and funerals—occasions for celebration and sorrow. The metaphor rebukes the spiritual stubbornness of Jesus' generation: they refused to respond to either John's somber call to repentance or Jesus' joyful invitation to the kingdom. Like petulant children demanding a different tune, they judged the message based on the mood of the messenger. This reveals a deeper issue: it wasn't the form that offended them, but the truth itself.

text: For John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, ‘He has a demon.’
‘The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say, ‘Look, a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners.’ Yet wisdom is justified by her deeds.’

footnotes.content: The accusation, “Look, a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners,” reflects how critics distorted Jesus' table fellowship to discredit him. “Glutton” and “drunkard” were serious moral charges in Jewish culture (cf. Deuteronomy 21:20), and the phrase “friend of sinners” was used derisively to question his holiness. Yet Jesus embraces the scandal of grace—he did not avoid the unclean, but brought the kingdom to them. The final line, “wisdom is justified by her children,” means that the fruit of his ministry—transformed lives—proves the divine origin of his actions. Truth is not measured by public approval, but by its lasting result.

text: Then he began to rebuke the cities where most of his mighty works had been done, because they did not repent.
‘Woe to you Chorazin, Woe to you Bethsaida! For if the miracles done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes.’
‘But I tell you, it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgment than for you.’
‘And you, Capernaum, will you be exalted to heaven? No, you will be brought down to Hades. For if the miracles done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained to this day.’

footnotes.content: Jesus rebukes Capernaum with language that echoes Isaiah 14:13–15, where the proud are cast down from heaven to Sheol. The word Hades translates the Greek Hadēs, referring to the realm of the dead—often associated with the Hebrew Sheol. It is not the final place of judgment (Gehenna), but a shadowed domain of spiritual exile and separation from the living. The contrast is sharp: though Capernaum had witnessed great miracles, it will not be exalted to divine glory, but will descend into death's domain. The warning signals both spiritual consequence and eschatological reversal—the proud will be brought low, and the unrepentant judged more severely than even Sodom.

text: ‘But I tell you, it will be more bearable for the land of Sodom on the day of judgment than for you.’
At that time, Jesus declared, ‘I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned and revealed them to little ones.’

footnotes.content: “Wise and understanding” refers not to true godly wisdom, but to those who regard themselves as intellectually competent—the educated, self-assured, or religiously elite who rely on their own insight rather than divine revelation. This phrase echoes Isaiah 29:14 and 1 Corinthians 1:19, where God nullifies the wisdom of the wise and the discernment of the discerning. The word translated “praise” (exomologoumai soi) does not mean to compliment or flatter, but to thank, confess, and acknowledge—expressing Jesus' deep agreement with the Father's hidden wisdom in revealing truth to the humble (“infants”) and concealing it from the self-reliant.

text: ‘Yes, Father, for this was your gracious will.’

footnotes.content: Jesus affirms that the Father's choice to reveal divine truths to the humble, rather than to the self-assured, was not random, but rooted in mercy. The Greek word eudokia can mean “good pleasure” or “delight,” but in this context, it points beyond mere satisfaction to the deeper benevolence of God's plan. “Gracious will” captures this nuance: what pleases God is not only wise, but kind — a will shaped by grace. Jesus celebrates not just the hiddenness of the kingdom, but the generosity behind its unveiling. This phrasing makes explicit the Father's joy in lifting the lowly and revealing truth to those open enough to receive it.

text: ‘All things have been handed over to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son wills to reveal Him.’

title: Come to Me, I Will Give You Rest

text: ‘Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.’
‘Share my labor upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.’
‘For my way is freeing and my burden is light.’

footnotes.content: The traditional phrasing "Take my yoke upon you" (v. 29) draws on a rich but now largely unfamiliar metaphor. In the first-century Jewish context, a "yoke" (zygos) referred not only to the wooden harness that bound two oxen together for plowing — a symbol of labor, constraint, and submission — but also to a rabbi's interpretive path, the ethical framework through which one lived out divine truth. This image carried profound spiritual weight, encompassing guidance, discipleship, relationship, empowerment, submission, partnership, liberation, comfort, fittingness, transformation, simplicity, grace, and peace. However, the physical yoke itself, though carefully shaped to fit the necks of the animals, still represented a binding and often burdensome reality. Even a well-carved yoke, intended to distribute the load evenly, still demanded effort and endurance, and an ill-fitting yoke could cause painful sores and strain, becoming a source of suffering. In the same way, a harsh or overly rigid religious system can crush the spirit and weary the soul, placing burdens too heavy to bear. By contrast, Jesus' path is one of grace and freedom, not mere external obligation. The TTT rendering "Share my labor upon you" (v. 29) and "For my way is freeing and my burden is light" (v. 30) seek to capture this liberating spirit without the cultural distance that the yoke metaphor can create today. By choosing "freeing" instead of the traditional "easy" (from the Greek chrēstos, meaning "kind" or "well-fitting"), this rendering emphasizes the release from guilt, shame, and religious oppression, inviting the weary soul into a life marked by divine companionship, transformative purpose, and shared strength. This approach aims to preserve the full depth of the original teaching while making it more immediately accessible to modern readers.

text: At that time, Jesus walked through grainfields one Sabbath. His disciples were hungry, so they plucked the heads of grain and ate.
The Pharisees objected, “Look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath!”
He said to them: “Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, and those beside him,
how he entered the house of God and ate the bread of the Presence, which only priests were permitted to eat?

footnotes.content: The phrase “bread of the Presence” refers to the 12 loaves that were set out continually before YHWH in the tabernacle and temple (see Leviticus 24:5–9). In Hebrew, it is called lechem ha-panim, literally “bread of the face” or “presence,” symbolizing covenant fellowship with God. Only priests were allowed to eat it under normal circumstances. By citing David's exception, Jesus highlights that human need can take precedence over ritual law—a principle grounded in the mercy of God rather than rigid legalism.

text: Or have you not read in the Law that the priests in the temple break the Sabbath and are still innocent?
But I tell you, something greater than the temple is here.

footnotes.content: In Second-Temple Judaism, the temple was the sacred dwelling place of God—central to worship, forgiveness, and identity. Jesus' statement that “something greater than the temple is here” would have shocked His hearers. He identifies Himself as the true meeting place between God and humanity, anticipating His role as the crucified and risen Son in whom God's presence fully dwells (cf. John 2:19–21; Colossians 2:9).

text: If you had known the meaning of “I desire mercy, not sacrifice,” you would not have condemned the innocent.
For the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.”

footnotes.content: When Jesus declares Himself “Lord of the Sabbath,” He asserts divine authority over one of the most sacred institutions in Jewish life. The Sabbath was instituted by YHWH at creation (Genesis 2:3) and codified in the Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:8–11). To claim lordship over it is to place Himself above Moses, above the Law, and even above time itself. This is not merely a statement of authority, but a revelation of His divine identity as the One who gave the Sabbath and now fulfills its deepest purpose—rest in God through Christ. The Greek phrase is kyrios tou sabbatou, which mirrors the authority title “Lord” used elsewhere for YHWH, intensifying the claim.

text: Jesus left there and entered their synagogue.
A man with a withered hand was there. They asked him, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?” hoping to accuse him.
He replied, “Who among you, if you had a sheep that fell into a pit on the Sabbath, would not take hold of it and lift it out?
Therefore, it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath.”
Then he said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He stretched it out, and it was fully restored like the other.
But the Pharisees went out and plotted how to destroy him.
Jesus, aware of this, withdrew. Many followed him, and he healed them all.
He warned them not to make him known,
to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah:
“Behold my chosen servant, my beloved in whom my soul is well-pleased. I will put my Spirit upon him, and he will proclaim justice to the nations.”
He will not argue or cry out, nor will his voice be heard in the streets.
A bruised reed he will not break, a smoldering wick he will not quench, until he leads justice to victory.
And in his name the nations will hope.
They brought him a demon-possessed man who was blind and mute. Jesus healed him, and the man spoke and saw.
The crowds were amazed and said, “Could this be the Son of David?”

footnotes.content: The title “Son of David” was widely recognized as a messianic term rooted in 2 Samuel 7:12–16 and Isaiah 11:1. The crowd's question reflects their amazement and rising hope that Jesus might be the promised King who would restore Israel. However, the phrasing in Greek (mēti houtos estin...?) implies hesitation or doubt—it can be read as “Surely this can't be...?” indicating their internal conflict between astonishment and disbelief.

text: But the Pharisees, seeing this, said, “This man casts out demons only by Beelzebul, the ruler of demons.”
Jesus, knowing their thoughts, said, “Every kingdom divided against itself is destroyed, and every house divided cannot stand.”
If Satan drives out Satan, he is divided against himself. How then can his kingdom stand?
If I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your own people cast them out? They will be your judges.

footnotes.content: Jesus refers to Jewish exorcists—perhaps disciples of the Pharisees or other traveling miracle workers—who were recognized as performing exorcisms. His argument exposes the inconsistency of accusing him of demonic collusion while approving their own. By saying “they will be your judges,” Jesus flips the accusation: their approval of other exorcists condemns their rejection of him.

text: But if I cast out demons by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.
How can anyone enter a strong man's house and take his possessions unless he first binds the strong man? Then he can plunder his house.
Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.
Therefore I tell you, every sin and blasphemy can be forgiven, but blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven.
Whoever speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, in this age or in the one to come.
Make the tree good, and its fruit will be good; make the tree bad, and its fruit will be bad, for a tree is known by its fruit.
You brood of vipers! How can you speak good, being evil? For out of the overflow of the heart, the mouth speaks.
A good person brings forth good from the treasure of a good heart, and an evil person brings forth evil from an evil heart.
But I tell you, every careless word people speak will be accounted for on the day of judgment.
For by your words you will be justified, and by your words you will be condemned.
Then some of the scribes and Pharisees said, “Teacher, we want to see a sign from you.”
He replied, “An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign, but none will be given except the sign of the prophet Jonah.
For just as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish for three days and three nights, so will the Son of Man be in the heart of the earth for three days and three nights.
The people of Nineveh will stand at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at Jonah's preaching, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.

The queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.”
When an unclean spirit leaves a person, it wanders through waterless places seeking rest but finds none.
Then it says, ‘I will return to my house from which I came.’ When it returns, it finds the house empty, swept, and put in order.

Then it goes and brings with it seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they enter and settle there. The final state of that person is worse than the first. So it will be with this wicked generation.
While he was still speaking to the crowds, his mother and brothers stood outside, wanting to speak with him.
Someone told him, “Look, your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to speak with you.”
But he replied to the one who told him, “Who is my mother, and who are my brothers?”
Stretching out his hand toward his disciples, he said, “Here are my mother and my brothers.
For whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother, and sister, and mother.”
That day, Jesus left the house and sat by the sea.
Large crowds gathered around him, so he got into a boat and sat down, while the crowd stood on the shore.
He spoke to them many things in parables: “A farmer went out to sow seed.”
As he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.
Other seed fell on rocky ground, where it sprang up quickly, having little soil.
But when the sun rose, it was scorched, and since it has no roots, it withered.
Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked it.
Yet other seed fell on good soil and produced a crop; some a hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.
Whoever has ears, let them listen.
The disciples came to him and asked, “Why do you speak to them in parables?”
He answered, “Because the secrets of the kingdom of heaven have been given to you, but not to them.”
For those who have will be given more, and they will have abundance. But those who do not have, even what they have will be taken away.

footnotes.content: The saying “For whoever has, more will be given… but whoever does not have, even what they have will be taken away” is a spiritual proverb Jesus repeats throughout the Gospels (cf. Matthew 25:29; Mark 4:25; Luke 8:18). It illustrates a divine principle of revelation: those who respond with faith and receptivity are given greater understanding, while those who resist lose even the truth they once held. In context, Jesus applies this to the parables—His disciples “have” because they seek, ask, and follow, so they are entrusted with the mysteries of the kingdom (v. 11). The crowds, however, remain passive or hardened, and thus truth is veiled. The Greek construction highlights this contrast of spiritual responsiveness. The word “have” does not refer to possessions, but to an open posture toward God's word. This principle echoes Proverbs 9:9, “Give instruction to a wise man, and he will be still wiser,” and is directly connected to Jesus' repeated warnings to listen with intent (cf. Mark 4:24; Luke 8:18).

text: This is why I speak to them in parables: Though seeing, they do not see; hearing, they do not listen or understand.
In them the prophecy of Isaiah is fulfilled: ‘You will hear and never understand; you will see and never perceive.’

For this people's hearts have grown hard; they barely hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise, they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn, and I would heal them.
But blessed are your eyes, for they see, and your ears, for they hear.
For truly I tell you, many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it.

title: The Parable of the Farmer Explained

text: Listen to what the parable of the farmer means.
When someone hears the word of the kingdom but does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in their heart. This is the seed along the path.
The seed on rocky ground is the one who hears the word and instantly receives it with joy,
but since it has no root, it lasts only a short time. When trouble or persecution comes because of the word, they quickly fall away.
The seed among thorns is the one who hears the word, but the worries of this age and the deceitfulness of wealth choke the word, making it unfruitful.
But the seed on good soil is the one who hears the word and understands it, bearing fruit, yielding a hundredfold, sixty, or thirty.
He presented another parable to them, saying, “The kingdom of heaven is like a farmer who sowed good seed in his field.”
But while everyone slept, his enemy came and sowed weeds among the wheat and went away.
When the seed sprouted and produced grain, the weeds also appeared.
The servants of the landowner came to him and said, ‘Master, didn't you sow good seed in your field? Where did these weeds come from?’
He replied, ‘An enemy did this.’ The servants asked, ‘Do you want us to pull them up?’
‘No,’ he said, ‘lest you uproot the wheat with them.’
Let both grow together until the harvest. Then I will tell the harvesters, ‘First gather the weeds and tie them in bundles to be burned, then gather the wheat into my barn.’

footnotes.content: Farmers burned darnel (a poisonous wheat look-alike) outside the village to prevent reseeding. Jesus uses this known harvest practice to frame the final judgment.

title: The Parables of the Mustard Seed and the Leaven

text: He presented another parable to them, saying, “The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed that a man planted in his field.”
Although it is the smallest of all seeds, when it grows, it becomes the largest of garden plants and forms a tree, and the birds of the air come and nest in its branches.

He told them another parable: “The kingdom of heaven is like yeast that a woman took and worked into three measures of flour, a large amount, until the whole batch of dough was fully leavened and risen.”

footnotes.content: The Greek word for “yeast” is zyme, which in many Jewish contexts symbolized corruption or impurity (cf. Exodus 12:15; Matthew 16:6). However, Jesus here reverses the symbol: the yeast represents the quiet, permeating power of the kingdom of heaven. Just as a small amount of leaven transforms a large batch of dough from within, so the kingdom spreads invisibly and irreversibly until it fills the whole. The image emphasizes hidden influence rather than external force.

title: Jesus Fulfills Prophecy by Speaking in Parables

text: Jesus spoke all these things to the crowds in parables. He said nothing to them without a parable,
to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet: “I will open my mouth in parables; I will declare things hidden since the foundation of the world.”

footnotes.content: The word zyme refers to fermented dough used as a rising agent. In Scripture, leaven often symbolizes hidden influence—typically negative (such as sin or hypocrisy), but here it is used positively to illustrate the transforming power of the kingdom of heaven. The “3 measures of flour” would yield enough bread to feed more than 100 people, emphasizing the kingdom's overwhelming effect. Though it begins invisibly and in small form, its impact quietly permeates everything it touches.

title: The Parable of the Weeds Explained

text: Then he left the crowds and went into the house. His disciples came to him and asked, “Explain to us the parable of the weeds in the field.”
He answered, “The man who sows the good seed is the Son of Man.”
The field is the world, and the good seed represents the children of the kingdom. The weeds are the children of the evil one,
and the enemy who sowed them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age, and the harvesters are angels.
Just as the weeds are gathered and burned in the fire, so it will be at the end of the age.
The Son of Man will send out his angels, and they will gather from his kingdom all causes of sin and the workers of lawlessness,
and throw them into the blazing furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Whoever has ears, let them listen.

title: The Hidden Treasure and the Pearl

text: The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field. When someone found it, they hid it again, and in their joy, they went and sold all they had and bought that field.
Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant seeking fine pearls.
When they found one of great value, they went away, sold all they had, and bought it.

footnotes.content: The phrase “hid again” reflects common legal practice in the ancient world. According to 1st-century Jewish and Roman law, buried treasure did not automatically belong to the finder—it was tied to ownership of the land. By hiding the treasure again, the person ensures they can legally acquire it by purchasing the field. The action highlights urgent joy and wise intent: giving up everything for what is of immeasurable worth.

text: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a net let down into the sea, gathering fish of every kind.
When it was full, they pulled it up on the shore, sat down, and collected the good fish into baskets, but the bad they threw away.
So it will be at the end of the age: the angels will come and separate the wicked from among the righteous
and throw them into the blazing furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Jesus said to them, "Have you understood all these things?" They replied, "Yes, Lord."
He said to them, "Therefore, every scribe trained for the kingdom of heaven is like the master of a house who brings out of his treasure both new and old things."

footnotes.content: The Greek word traditionally translated “scribe” (grammateus) referred to someone trained in the Torah—an expert in Jewish law. By saying “becomes a disciple,” Jesus implies that even these respected teachers must undergo transformation and submit to the new reality of the kingdom. The image of bringing out “new and old” treasures points to their role in weaving together the wisdom of the Hebrew Scriptures with the fresh revelation found in Christ. The kingdom does not discard the old—it fulfills it.

text: When Jesus had finished these parables, he left that place.
Coming to his hometown, he taught them in their synagogue, and they were astonished, saying, "Where did this man get this wisdom and these mighty works?"
Is not this the carpenter's son? Is not his mother called Mary, and his brothers James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas?
And are not all his sisters with us? Where then did this man get all these things?
And they took offense at him. But Jesus said to them, "A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown and in his own household."

footnotes.content: The phrase “took offense” translates the Greek skandalizō, from which we get the word “scandal.” It means to stumble over, to be repelled by, or to reject because of a perceived contradiction or unacceptability. The townspeople could not reconcile Jesus' divine wisdom and miracles with their familiarity with him. This paradox—divine glory clothed in local ordinariness—provoked contempt rather than faith.

text: And he did not do many miracles there because of their unbelief.

title: The Death of John the Baptist

text: At that time, Herod the tetrarch heard the news about Jesus
and said to his servants, “This is John the Baptist. He has risen from the dead, and that is why these miraculous powers are at work in him.”
For Herod had arrested John, bound him, and put him in prison because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife,
because John had kept telling him, “It is not lawful for you to have her.”

footnotes.content: John's rebuke comes from the Torah, specifically Leviticus 18:16 and 20:21, where taking a brother's wife is forbidden. Herod's act was seen as both adulterous and unlawful by Jewish standards.

text: Although he wanted to kill him, he feared the people, for they considered John a prophet.
But on Herod's birthday, the daughter of Herodias danced before the company and pleased Herod,

footnotes.content: The daughter of Herodias is unnamed in the Gospels, but known from Josephus as Salome. Her dance, likely suggestive in nature, exemplifies the decadence of Herod's court.

text: so he promised with an oath to give her whatever she wished.
At her mother's urging, she said, “Give me the head of John the Baptist here on a platter.”
Although the king was distressed, because of his oaths and guests, he ordered it to be given.
He sent and had John beheaded in prison.
His head was brought on a platter, given to the girl, and she took it to her mother.
John's disciples came, took the body, buried it, then went and reported to Jesus.
When Jesus heard this, he withdrew by boat to a solitary place, but the crowds, hearing of it, followed him on foot from the towns.

footnotes.content: The Greek verb anachōreō means to withdraw, often for solitude or prayer. Jesus likely sought quiet after hearing of John's death.

text: When he came ashore, he saw a great crowd, felt compassion for them, and healed their sick.

footnotes.content: “Was moved with compassion” translates the verb esplagchnisthē, expressing deep, gut-level divine mercy—not mere pity.

text: As evening approached, the disciples came to him and said, “This is a remote place, and the hour is late. Send the crowds away to the villages to buy food for themselves.”
But Jesus said, “They do not need to go away. You give them something to eat.”
They said, “We have only five loaves and two fish.”
He said, “Bring them here to me.”
He instructed the crowds to sit on the grass. Taking the five loaves and two fish, he looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke them, and gave them to the disciples, who shared them with the crowds.

footnotes.content: Jesus' actions—blessing, breaking, giving—foreshadow the Last Supper. The parallel verbs link daily provision with sacrificial meaning.

text: They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples gathered twelve full baskets of leftovers.
Those who ate were about five thousand men, besides women and children.

footnotes.content: The word “men” is from the Greek andres, meaning adult males. The crowd total, including women and children, likely exceeded 10,000.

text: Immediately, Jesus made his disciples get into the boat and go ahead of him to the other side, while he dismissed the crowds.

footnotes.content: The Greek verb ēnankasen means 'to compel' or 'to strongly urge.' “Urgently” matches the forcefulness of the moment better than “immediately”.

text: Right after he went up on the mountain by himself to pray. When evening came, he was there alone.
Meanwhile, the boat was already in the middle of the sea, battered by the waves, for the wind was against it.
In the fourth watch of the night, Jesus came to them, walking on the sea.

footnotes.content: “Fourth watch” refers to 3–6 a.m., the last Roman division of the night—emphasizing exhaustion and divine timing.

text: When the disciples saw him walking on the sea, they were terrified and said, “It is a ghost!” and cried out in fear.
But immediately Jesus said to them, “Take heart! It is I. Do not be afraid.”

footnotes.content: “It is I” translates egō eimi, which can bear divine overtones. Here, it provides assurance rather than a theophanic claim.

text: Peter answered, “Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water.”
“Come,” he said. Then Peter got down out of the boat, walked on the water, and came toward Jesus.
But when he noticed the strong wind, he was afraid and, beginning to sink, cried out, “Lord, save me!”

footnotes.content: The verb katapontizesthai means to sink deeply—used here to portray Peter's physical and spiritual faltering.

text: Immediately, Jesus reached out his hand, took hold of him, and said, “You of little faith, why did you doubt?”
And when they climbed into the boat, the wind ceased.
Then those in the boat came and worshiped him, saying, “Truly, you are the Son of God.”
When they had crossed over, they came to the region of Gennesaret.
When the people of that place recognized him, they sent word throughout the surrounding region and brought to him all who were sick.
They begged him to let them touch just the edge of his cloak, and all who touched it were healed.

footnotes.content: “Fringe of his garment” refers to the tzitzit, or tassels commanded in Numbers 15:38. The Greek verb diasōthēsan means to be delivered, healed, or made whole.

text: Then the scribes and Pharisees from Jerusalem came to Jesus, saying:
“Why do your disciples break the tradition of the elders? They do not wash their hands before they eat.”
He answered, “And why do you break the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition?”
For God said, ‘Honor your father and mother,’ and, ‘Whoever curses father or mother must be put to death.’
But you say, ‘If anyone declares to their parents, “Whatever support you might have received from me is a gift devoted to God,”
they are not obligated to honor their father or mother.’ You have nullified the word of God for the sake of your tradition.

footnotes.content: Jesus rebukes them for using human traditions—like the “Corban” vow—to nullify God's clear command to honor and care for one's parents. By declaring resources “devoted to God,” they avoided supporting their parents while still appearing righteous. His charge exposes how pious-sounding customs were weaponized to override divine law.

text: Hypocrites! Isaiah rightly prophesied about you, saying:
‘This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.
In vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men.’

footnotes.content: “Commandments of men” refers to teachings created by altering God's actual commandments—either by changing the words or redefining their meaning. These man-made rules were treated as divine truth, but Jesus declares such worship empty.

text: He called the crowd and said, “Listen and understand:
It is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person; but what comes out of the mouth;this defiles a person.”
Then his disciples came and said, “Do you know the Pharisees were offended by this statement?”
He answered, “Every plant my Father has not planted will be uprooted.”
“Leave them alone. They are blind guides leading the blind, and if the blind lead the blind, both will fall into a pit.”
Then Peter said, “Explain this parable to us.”
Jesus said, “Are you also still without understanding?”
“Do you not see that whatever enters the mouth goes into the stomach and is expelled?”
“But what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart, and this defiles a person.”
“For out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, and slander.”
“These are what defile a person, but eating with unwashed hands does not defile a person.”

title: The Faith of a Canaanite Woman

text: Jesus left there and went to the region of Tyre and Sidon.
A Canaanite woman from that region came out, crying, “Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me! My daughter is severely tormented by a demon.”
But he did not answer her. His disciples urged him, “Send her away, for she keeps crying out after us.”
He replied, “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.”
But she came and knelt before him, saying, “Lord, help me!”
He replied, “It is not fitting to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “household dogs” translates the Greek word kynaria, a diminutive form of kyōn (“dog”), which refers to small, domestic animals kept in the home—not wild scavengers. In Jewish culture, “dogs” often symbolized Gentiles as outsiders to the covenant. Jesus' use of the softened term reflects a testing tone rather than an insult. By framing Gentile access as indirect (“crumbs”), He both affirms Israel's covenant priority and opens space for persistent faith to be honored. Her response reveals spiritual insight and humility, and Jesus' approval marks her as a forerunner of Gentile inclusion in the kingdom.

text: “Yes, Lord,” she said, “but even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table.”
Then Jesus said, “Dear woman, great is your faith! Let it be done for you as you desire.” And her daughter was healed from that very hour.
Jesus walked along the Sea of Galilee, went up the mountain, and sat down.
Great crowds came to him, bringing the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others. They laid them at his feet, and he healed them.
The crowd marveled as they saw the mute speaking, the crippled made whole, the lame walking, and the blind seeing. And they praised the God of Israel.

Then Jesus called his disciples and said, “I have compassion for the crowd, because they have already been with me three days and have nothing to eat. I do not want to send them away hungry, lest they collapse on the way.”
His disciples said, “Where can we get enough bread in this desolate place to feed such a crowd?”
Jesus asked, “How many loaves do you have?” They replied, “Seven, and a few small fish.”
He instructed the crowd to sit on the ground.
Taking the seven loaves and the fish, he gave thanks, broke them, and gave them to the disciples, who shared them with the crowd.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples collected seven full baskets of leftovers.
Those who ate were four thousand men, besides women and children.
After sending the crowd away, he got into the boat and went to the region of Magadan.
The Pharisees and Sadducees approached to test Him, asking for a sign from heaven.
He replied, “When evening comes, you say, ‘Fair weather, for the sky is red,’
and in the morning, ‘Stormy weather, for the sky is red and overcast.’ You know how to interpret the sky but fail to discern the signs of the times.
An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign, but none will be given except the sign of Jonah.” Then he left them and went away.

footnotes.content: The term 'adulterous generation' refers to a spiritually unfaithful people, echoing the language of the Old Testament prophets like Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel, who described Israel's idolatry and covenant betrayal as spiritual adultery. In this context, Jesus is condemning the generation that rejects his message despite the clear evidence of his divine authority. The 'sign of Jonah' refers to Jonah's three days in the belly of the great fish (Jonah 1:17), foreshadowing Jesus' own death, burial, and resurrection. It is the only sign Jesus offers as proof to those demanding miraculous confirmation, emphasizing faith in his resurrection over spectacle.

text: When the disciples crossed to the other side, they forgot to bring bread.
Jesus said to them, “Watch and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
They discussed this among themselves, saying, “It is because we did not bring any bread.”
Aware of this, Jesus said, “You of little faith, why are you talking about having no bread?
Do you still not understand? Do you not remember the five loaves for the five thousand, and the full baskets you gathered?
Or the seven loaves for the four thousand, and how many baskets you gathered?
How do you not understand that I was not speaking about bread? Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
Then they understood that he was not talking about bread, but about the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

footnotes.content: ‘Leaven’ was a common Jewish metaphor for influence or corruption. Jesus used it here to refer not to physical bread but to the doctrinal distortion and spiritual hypocrisy of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

title: The Rock Declares Jesus as the Christ

text: When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples, “Who do people say the Son of Man is?”
They replied, “Some say John the Baptist, others Elijah, and still others Jeremiah or one of the prophets.”
He said to them, “But who do you say I am?”
Simon Peter answered, “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.”
Jesus replied, “Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah, for this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my Father in heaven.
And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it.

footnotes.content: Jesus' statement, 'You are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church,' includes a deliberate Greek wordplay between 'Petros' ('stone') and 'petra' ('rock' or 'bedrock').

text: I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven; whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.”

footnotes.content: The 'keys of the kingdom of heaven' represent spiritual authority to open and shut access, drawn from Jewish imagery (cf. Isaiah 22:22).

text: Then he commanded his disciples not to tell anyone that he was the Christ.
From that time, Jesus began to tell his disciples that he must go to Jerusalem, suffer many things from the elders, chief priests, and scribes, be killed, and be raised on the third day.
Then Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him, saying, “Far be it from you, Lord! This will never happen to you!”
But Jesus turned and said to Peter, “Get behind me, Satan! You are a stumbling block to me, for you are not mindful of the things of God, but the things of people.”

footnotes.content: The phrase 'merely human concerns' refers to thoughts shaped by natural instinct, fear, or worldly reasoning.

text: Then Jesus said to his disciples, “If anyone desires to come after me, they must deny themselves, take up their cross, and follow me.”
For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for my sake will find it.

footnotes.content: The Greek word for 'life' is 'psychē,' which also means 'soul,' referring to one's eternal self.

text: For what will it profit someone to gain the whole world if they lose their soul? Or what can someone give in exchange for their soul?

footnotes.content: The phrase 'forfeit their soul' uses the Greek verb 'zēmiōthēnai,' meaning to suffer loss, be penalized, or forfeit.

text: For the Son of Man will come in the glory of his Father with his angels, and then he will reward each one according to their deeds.
Truly, I tell you, some standing here will not taste death until they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.

footnotes.content: This verse has several interpretations, including the Transfiguration, the resurrection, Pentecost, or the 70 AD judgment on Jerusalem.

text: After six days, Jesus took Peter, James, and his brother John up a high mountain alone.
He was transfigured before them; his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as bright as light.
And behold, Moses and Elijah appeared, talking with him.
Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here; if you wish, let us set up three shelters: one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.”
While he was still speaking, behold, a bright cloud overshadowed them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased. Listen to him!”
When the disciples heard this, they fell facedown to the ground, terrified.
But Jesus came, touched them, and said, “Rise, do not be afraid.”
When they lifted up their eyes, they saw no one but Jesus alone.
As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus commanded them, “Tell no one about the vision until the Son of Man is risen from the dead.”
His disciples asked him, “Why then do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?”
Jesus replied, “Indeed, Elijah comes first and will restore all things.”
“But I tell you, Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him but did to him however they wished. In the same way, the Son of Man will also suffer at their hands.”
Then the disciples understood that he was speaking to them about John the Baptist.
When they came to the crowd, a man approached Jesus, knelt before him, and said,
“Lord, have mercy on my son, for he is an epileptic, suffers terribly, and often falls into fire or water.”

footnotes.content: In ancient Jewish and Greco-Roman contexts, epilepsy was often attributed to lunar influence—hence the Greek word selēniazetai (“moonstruck”). The mention of “fire and water” reflects common household dangers of the time: open flames for cooking and open cisterns or wells for water. The boy's seizures placed him at risk of falling into these hazards, reinforcing both the physical and spiritual urgency of his condition.

text: “I brought him to your disciples, but they could not heal him.”
Jesus said, “You faithless and twisted generation, how long must I be with you? How long must I bear with you? Bring him here to me.”

footnotes.content: Jesus' lament echoes the prophetic language of Deuteronomy 32:5, where Israel is called a “twisted generation” for turning away from God despite witnessing His works. The Greek words translated “faithless and twisted” express not just doubt, but a refusal to trust and a distortion of heart and mind away from truth. Though the rebuke applies broadly to the surrounding generation, it also includes the disciples, who had just failed to cast out the demon due to their lack of faith (v.20). Jesus speaks with sorrowful urgency, not condemnation—grieved by a people who have seen much, yet still resist belief. His frustration reflects the burden of the Messiah who walks among a spiritually misaligned people, longing to restore their faith.

text: Jesus rebuked the demon, and it came out of him, and the boy was healed from that very hour.
Then the disciples came to Jesus privately and asked, “Why could we not cast it out?”

Because of your little faith. Truly I tell you, if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you can say to this mountain, ‘Move from here to there,’ and it will move. Nothing will be impossible for you.
[“But this kind only comes out by prayer and fasting.”]
While they were staying in Galilee, Jesus said to them, “The Son of Man is about to be betrayed into the hands of men,”
“and they will kill him, and on the third day he will be raised up.” And they were deeply grieved.
When they came to Capernaum, the collectors of the temple tax approached Peter and asked, “Does your teacher not pay the temple tax?”

He replied, “Yes.” When he entered the house, Jesus anticipated him, saying, “What do you think, Simon? From whom do the kings of the earth collect taxes or customs, from their own sons or from outsiders?”
Peter answered, “From outsiders.” Jesus said to him, “Then the sons are free.”

footnotes.content: Jesus' question teaches that, just as royal children are exempt from taxes levied by their father, the Son of God is not obligated to pay temple tax to His own Father. His point is that the temple ultimately belongs to God—and therefore to Him. Yet, for the sake of peace and to avoid offense, He chooses to pay.

text: “But so we do not offend them, go to the sea, cast a hook, and take the first fish you catch. When you open its mouth, you will find a four-drachma coin. Take it and give it to them for me and you.”

footnotes.content: The “four-drachma coin” refers to a silver coin called a statēr, worth 4 drachmas—the exact amount needed to pay the temple tax for 2 people (2 drachmas each). This value corresponds to 1 shekel, as described in Exodus 30:13, where every male was required to pay a half-shekel as atonement money. Although the Old Testament uses the term “shekel,” Greek-speaking Jews in the 1st century often paid the tax using drachma-based currency minted in Tyre or elsewhere.

text: At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”
He called a child and placed the child in their midst.
He said, “Truly, I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.”
Therefore, whoever humbles themselves like this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.
And whoever receives a little one like this child in my name receives me.
But whoever causes any of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for them to have a millstone hung around their neck and be drowned in the depths of the sea.
Woe to the world for the temptations it causes, for such things are inevitable, but doomed is the person through whom they come!

footnotes.content: The Greek term ouai is commonly rendered as “woe” in most English translations, but this term can be archaic and unclear in its precise intention on first read. In this verse, it captures both divine sorrow and severe judgment. The first occurrence was rendered as “grief” to emphasize the layered compassion and lament Jesus felt for a world that inevitably produces temptations. The second occurrence was rendered as “doomed” to reflect the certainty of divine judgment upon those who cause others to stumble, as described in verse 6, where such a person is said to be better off drowned with a millstone around their neck, emphasizing the irreversible nature of their condemnation.

text: If your hand or foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into the eternal fire.
And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of Gehenna.

title: The Parable of the Lost Sheep

text: See that you do not despise one of these little ones, for I tell you, their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father in heaven.
For the Son of Man has come to save the lost.
What do you think? If a man has a hundred sheep and one of them goes astray, will he not leave the ninety‑nine on the hills and go to find the one that went astray?
And if he finds it, truly I tell you, he rejoices more over that one than over the ninety‑nine that did not stray.
In the same way, it is not the will of your Father in heaven that any of these little ones should perish.

title: Confronting a Dear One in Sin

text: If a dear one sins against you, go and point out their fault between you and them alone. If they listen to you, you have won them over.
But if they will not listen, take one or two others along, so that every accusation may be confirmed by the testimony of two or three witnesses.
If they refuse to listen to them, share it with the church. And if they refuse to listen even to the church, treat them as you would a Gentile or a tax collector.
Truly I tell you, whatever you restrain on earth will be restrained in heaven, and whatever you set free on earth will be set free in heaven.
Again, truly I tell you, if two of you agree on earth about anything they ask, it will be done for them by my Father in heaven.
For where two or three gather in my name, there I am in their midst.

title: The Parable of the Unforgiving Servant

text: Then Peter came to him and asked, “Lord, how many times should I forgive a dear one if they sin against me? Up to seven times?”
Jesus said to him, “I do not say up to seven times, but up to seventy times seven.”
Therefore, the kingdom of heaven is like a king who wanted to settle accounts with his servants.
As he began to settle, a servant who owed him ten thousand talents was brought to him.

footnotes.content: The Greek term for “talent” (talanton) refers to the largest unit of currency in the ancient world, often representing about 6,000 denarii. In this parable, ten thousand talents would equate to approximately 60 million days' wages, an impossibly large debt intended to symbolize the vast, unpayable nature of human sin before God. The exaggeration highlights the incomprehensible grace and forgiveness of the king, representing God.

text: Since he could not pay, his master ordered that he, his wife, his children, and all he had be sold to repay the debt.
The servant fell down before him and said, ‘Master, have patience with me, and I will repay you everything.’
The master of that servant took pity on him, released him, and forgave the debt.
But that servant went out and found one of his fellow servants who owed him a hundred denarii. He seized him and began to choke him, saying, ‘Pay me what you owe!’

footnotes.content: A denarius (denarion) was the typical daily wage for a laborer in Roman times. One hundred denarii, therefore, represents roughly 100 days' wages—substantial but manageable compared to the astronomical debt of ten thousand talents. The contrast emphasizes the servant's unwillingness to extend even a small portion of the forgiveness he received.

text: His fellow servant fell at his feet and begged him, ‘Have patience with me, and I will repay you.’
But he refused and threw him into prison until he could pay back what he owed.
When his fellow servants saw what happened, they were deeply grieved and went to their master and reported all that had happened.
Then his master called him and said, ‘You wicked servant! I forgave all that debt because you begged me.’
Should you not have had mercy on your fellow servant, just as I had mercy on you?
In his anger, his master handed him over to be tortured until he paid all he owed.

footnotes.content: The Greek word used here, basanistais, implies more than just imprisonment; it indicates being handed over to tormentors or torturers. This severe consequence highlights the gravity of the servant's unforgiveness, emphasizing that divine judgment on those who refuse to forgive is not merely punitive but involves suffering proportionate to the offense.

text: So also my Father in heaven will do to you if each of you does not forgive your brothers and sisters from the heart.
When Jesus had finished these sayings, he left Galilee and went into the region of Judea beyond the Jordan.
Great crowds followed him, and he healed them there.
Some Pharisees approached to test him and asked, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any reason?”
He answered, “Have you not read that the one who created them from the beginning made them male and female?”
and said, “For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh?
Then they are no longer two, but one flesh. Therefore, what God has joined together, let no one divide.”
They said, “Why then did Moses command to give a certificate of divorce and send her away?”
He answered, “Moses permitted you to divorce your wives because of the hardness of your hearts, but it was not so from the beginning.”
I tell you, any man who divorces his wife, except for sexual covenant treachery, and marries another, commits adultery. And any man who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
His disciples said to him, “If this is the case between a man and his wife, it is better not to marry.”
Jesus replied, “Not everyone can accept this word, but only those to whom it has been given.

For there are eunuchs who were born this way, and there are eunuchs made so by others, and there are eunuchs who made themselves so for the sake of the kingdom of heaven. Let the one who can accept this teaching receive it.”
Then little children were brought to him that he might lay his hands on them and pray, but the disciples rebuked them.
But Jesus said, “Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these.”
And he laid his hands on them and departed from there.
Then someone came to him and asked, “Good Teacher, what good thing must I do to have eternal life?”
So he said to him, “Why do you call me good? No one is good but One, that is, God. But if you want to enter into the life of God, keep the commandments.”

footnotes.content: The phrase "enter into the life of God" reflects the deeper theological concept behind the Greek term zōē aiōnios, which signifies not merely endless existence but a profound participation in the divine nature, as emphasized in passages like 2 Peter 1:4 and John 17:3. This understanding aligns with the broader biblical vision of eternal life as living in intimate relationship with God, beyond mere duration.

text: He asked, “Which ones?” Jesus replied, “‘You shall not murder,’ ‘You shall not commit adultery,’ ‘You shall not steal,’ ‘You shall not bear false witness,’
‘Honor your father and mother,’ and, ‘You shall love your neighbor as yourself.’”
The young man said to him, “All these I have kept from my youth. What do I still lack?”
Jesus said to him, “If you want to be perfect, go, sell your possessions and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me.”
When the young man heard this, he went away sorrowful, for he had many possessions.
Then Jesus said to his disciples, “Truly, I tell you, it is hard for a rich person to enter the kingdom of heaven.
Again, I tell you, it is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.”
When the disciples heard this, they were profoundly dismayed and said, “Who then can be saved?”
But Jesus looked at them and said, “With people this is impossible, but with God all things are possible.”
Then Peter answered him, “Look, we have left everything and followed you. What then will we have?”

Jesus said to them, “Truly, I tell you, at the renewal of all things, when the Son of Man sits on his glorious throne, you who have followed me will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.
And everyone who has left houses, brothers, sisters, father, mother, children, or lands for my name's sake will receive a hundredfold and will inherit eternal life.
But many who are first will be last, and the last first.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “many who are first will be last, and the last first” captures a recurring theme in Jesus' teachings about the reversal of worldly status in the kingdom of God. This principle emphasizes that earthly measures of greatness, privilege, and rank often hold little value in God's economy, where humility, service, and self-sacrifice are exalted. It echoes themes from the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) and the parable of the workers in the vineyard (Matthew 20:1-16), illustrating that divine reward is based on God's grace rather than human merit or status. The underlying Greek phrase, eschaton esontai prōtoi, reflects a radical inversion of human expectations.

title: The Parable of the Workers in the Vineyard

text: “The kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out early in the morning to hire workers for his vineyard.”
“He agreed with the workers for one denarius for the day and sent them into his vineyard.”
“About the third hour he went out, saw others standing idle in the marketplace,”
and said, ‘You also go into the vineyard, and I will pay you what is just.’ So they went.
He went out again about the sixth and the ninth hour and did the same.
About the eleventh hour he found others standing and said, ‘Why are you standing here all day doing nothing?’
They answered, ‘Because no one has hired us.’ He said, ‘You also go into the vineyard, and you will receive what is just.’
When evening came, the owner of the vineyard told his foreman, ‘Call the workers and pay them their wages, beginning with the last and ending with the first.’
The ones hired at the eleventh hour came and each received a denarius.
When the first came, they expected more, yet they also received a denarius.
On receiving it, they grumbled against the landowner,
saying, ‘These last worked only one hour, and you have made them equal to us who bore the burden of the day and the scorching heat.’
But he answered one of them, ‘Friend, I am not wronging you. Did you not agree with me for a denarius?’
‘Take what is yours and go. I choose to give to this last as I gave to you.’
‘Am I not free to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous?’
Thus the last will be first and the first last. For many are called, but few are chosen.

footnotes.content: This closing statement, “Therefore, the last will be first, and the first last,” reflects a recurring theme in Jesus' teaching about the reversal of worldly status in the kingdom of heaven. It emphasizes that divine reward is based not on the duration or apparent prominence of one's work but on the grace and generosity of God. This parable challenges human notions of fairness, illustrating that God's ways are not bound by human calculations but reflect a radically generous and merciful order (cf. Matthew 19:30, Luke 13:30).

title: Jesus Predicts His Death a Third Time

text: As Jesus was going up to Jerusalem, he took the twelve aside on the road and said,
“Take heart, we are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered to the chief priests and scribes; they will condemn him to death”
“and hand him over to the Gentiles to mock, flog, and crucify him; and on the third day he will rise.”
Then the mother of Zebedee's sons came to him with her sons, kneeling and asking a favor of him.
He said to her, “What do you want?” She said, “Say that these two sons of mine may sit, one at your right hand and one at your left, in your kingdom.”
Jesus replied, “You do not know what you are asking. Are you able to drink the cup I am about to drink?” They said to him, “We are able.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “baptism I am about to undergo (by blood)” refers to the profound suffering and sacrificial death Jesus was soon to face. In this context, “baptism” symbolizes the complete immersion into suffering and death, a common metaphor in both Jewish and early Christian thought (cf. Luke 12:50, Mark 10:38-39). This “baptism” highlights the totality of his sacrifice, not just physical suffering but the full weight of spiritual and redemptive purpose.

text: He said to them, “You will indeed drink my cup, but to sit at my right and left is not mine to grant; it is for those for whom my Father has prepared it.”
When the ten heard, they were indignant with the two brothers.

footnotes.content: The term “indignant” in this verse captures a deeper sense of moral outrage among the ten disciples. The Greek term aganakteō (ἀγανακτέω) conveys a strong, almost righteous anger at perceived unfairness or presumption. This response likely arose from a sense of betrayal or favoritism, as the request for special status directly contradicted the servant-hearted values Jesus had repeatedly emphasized (cf. Matthew 18:1-4, Mark 10:41).

text: But Jesus called them and said, “You know that the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and the great exercise authority over them.”
“It shall not be so among you. Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant,”
“and whoever wants to be first among you must be your slave,”
“just as the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve and to give his life as a ransom for many.”
As they were leaving Jericho, a great crowd followed him.
Two blind men sitting by the road, hearing that Jesus was passing, cried out, “Have mercy on us, Lord, Son of David!”
The crowd rebuked them to be silent, but they cried out all the louder, “Have mercy on us, Lord, Son of David!”
Jesus stopped, called them, and said, “What do you want me to do for you?”
They said to him, “Lord, that our eyes may be opened.”
Moved with compassion, Jesus touched their eyes; immediately they regained sight and followed him.
As they approached Jerusalem and came to Bethphage at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples,
He told them, “Go into the village ahead of you, and right there you will find a donkey tied, and a colt with her. Untie them and bring them to me.
If anyone asks you, ‘Why are you doing this?’ say, ‘The Lord needs them,’ and he will send them right away.”
This happened to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet:
“Say to the daughter of Zion: ‘Look, your King is coming to you, humble and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.’”

footnotes.content: The phrase “the foal of a donkey” is a reference to a young, unbroken donkey, emphasizing the humility and peace of the Messiah's approach, in contrast to the war horses typically ridden by conquering kings. This echoes the prophecy of Zechariah 9:9, symbolizing a peaceful, righteous ruler rather than a military conqueror.

text: So the disciples went and did as Jesus instructed them.
They brought the donkey and the colt, placed their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on them.
A large crowd spread their cloaks on the road, while others cut branches from the trees and spread them on the path.
The crowds that went ahead and those that followed shouted: “Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest!”
When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the whole city was stirred and asked, “Who is this?”
The crowds replied, “This is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee.”
Jesus entered the temple and drove out all who were buying and selling. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the benches of those selling doves.
He said to them, “It is written, ‘My house will be called a house of prayer,’ but you have made it a den of thieves.”
Then the blind and the lame came to him at the temple, and he healed them.
But when the chief priests and scribes saw the wonderful things he did, and the children shouting in the temple, “Hosanna to the Son of David!”, they were indignant
They said to him, “Do you hear what these children are saying?” Jesus replied, “Yes. Have you never read, ‘Out of the mouths of children and infants you have perfected praise’?”

footnotes.content: The phrase “you have perfected praise” is a direct quotation from Psalm 8:2 in the Greek Septuagint, where the word katartizō (perfected) carries the sense of establishing, completing, or making fully ready. It emphasizes the idea that even the simplest, most unpolished voices can offer pure and complete praise, reflecting God's ability to bring divine purpose to full expression through humble vessels.

text: Then he left them and went out of the city to Bethany, where he spent the night.
In the morning, as he returned to the city, he became hungry.
Seeing a fig tree by the road, he went to it but found nothing except leaves. He said to it, “May you never bear fruit ever again.” And at once, the fig tree withered.
When the disciples saw this, they were astonished and said, “How did the fig tree wither so quickly?”

Jesus answered, “Truly, I tell you, if you have faith and do not doubt, not only will you do what was done to the fig tree, but even if you say to this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,’ it will happen.
And anything you ask in prayer, believing, you will receive.”
When Jesus entered the temple, the chief priests and the elders confronted him as he was teaching and said, “By what authority are you doing these things, and who gave you this authority?”
Jesus replied, “I will also ask you one question, and if you answer me, I will tell you by what authority I do these things.
The baptism of John; where was it from? From heaven or from people?” They discussed this among themselves, saying, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ he will ask, ‘Then why did you not believe him?’
But if we say, ‘From people,’ we fear the crowd, for they all regard John as a prophet.”
So they answered Jesus, “We do not know.” Then he said, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.”

title: The Parable of the Two Sons

text: “What do you think? A man had two sons. He went to the first and said, ‘Son, go and work today in my vineyard.’
The son answered, ‘I will not,’ but later he regretted it and went.
Then the man went to the second son and said the same. He answered, ‘I will, sir,’ but he did not go.
Which of the two did the will of his father?” They replied, “The first.” Jesus said, “Truly, I tell you, tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God ahead of you.
For John came to you in the way of righteousness, but you did not believe him. Yet tax collectors and prostitutes believed him, and even after you saw this, you did not repent and believe him.”

title: The Parable of the Wicked Tenants

text: Listen to another parable: There was a landowner who planted a vineyard, put a hedge around it, dug a winepress, and built a tower. Then he leased it to tenants and went away.
When the harvest approached, he sent his servants to the tenants to collect his fruit.
But the tenants seized his servants. They beat one, killed another, and stoned a third.
Again, he sent more servants than the first, and they treated them the same way.
Then last of all he sent his son to them, saying, ‘They will respect my son.’
But when the tenants saw the son, they said to each other, ‘This is the heir. Come, let us kill him and take his inheritance.’
So they seized him, threw him out of the vineyard, and killed him.
So when the owner of the vineyard comes, what will he do to those tenants?
They replied, “He will destroy those wicked tenants in a terrible way and lease the vineyard to others who will give him the fruits in their season.”
Jesus said to them, “Have you never read in the Scriptures: ‘The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes.’

footnotes.content: The phrase “the stone the builders rejected” is a direct reference to Psalm 118:22-23, which originally described the surprising victory of a seemingly insignificant stone becoming the most critical part of the structure. In the context of this parable, Jesus identifies himself as the cornerstone, rejected by the religious leaders but chosen by God to be the foundation of the new covenant. The term “marvelous” captures both the unexpected nature and the divine significance of this reversal, aligning with the broader biblical theme of God's wisdom confounding human expectations.

text: Therefore, I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken from you and given to a people who will produce its fruit.
And whoever falls on this stone will be broken, but on whomever it falls, it will crush them to pieces.
When the chief priests and Pharisees heard his parables, they realized he was speaking about them.
But when they sought to arrest him, they feared the crowds, because they considered him to be a prophet.”

title: The Parable of the Wedding Feast

text: Jesus spoke to them again in parables, saying,
“The kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding feast for his son.
He sent his servants to call those invited to the feast, but they refused to come.
Again, he sent other servants, saying, ‘Tell those invited: “Behold, I have prepared my feast; my oxen and fattened cattle are slaughtered; everything is ready. Come to the wedding feast.”’
But they disregarded the invitation; one went to his farm, another to his business.
The others seized his servants, abused them, and killed them.
When the king heard this, he was enraged. He sent his armies, destroyed those murderers, and burned up their city.
Then he said to his servants, ‘The wedding feast is ready, but those invited were not worthy.
So go into the highways and invite everyone you find to the wedding.’
The servants went out into the highways and gathered all they found, both good and bad, and the wedding hall was filled with guests.
But when the king came to see the guests, he noticed a man without a wedding garment.
He said to him, ‘Friend, how did you get in here without a wedding garment?’ The man was speechless.
Then the king said to the servants, ‘Bind him hand and foot and throw him into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.’

footnotes.content: The wedding garment in this parable symbolizes the righteousness required to enter the kingdom of heaven, not merely an invitation. In the cultural context, it was customary for a host to provide garments for guests, making the absence of one a sign of rejection or presumption. The phrase “outer darkness” reflects ultimate exclusion from divine presence, characterized by grief and despair. The image of “weeping and gnashing of teeth” further underscores the severity of this separation, capturing profound regret and irreversible loss.

text: For many are called, but only a few are chosen.”
Then the Pharisees went and plotted how to trap him in his words.

They sent their disciples to him, along with the Herodians, saying, “Teacher, we know you are truthful and teach the way of God with integrity. You are not swayed by others, for you do not show partiality.
So tell us then, what do you think? Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not?”
But Jesus, aware of their malice, said, “Why are you testing me, you hypocrites?
Show me the coin used for the tax.” So they brought him a denarius.
He asked them, “Whose image and inscription is this?”
They said, “Caesar's.” Then he said to them, “Give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's.”
When they heard this, they were amazed, and they left him and went away.
That same day the Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, came to him and questioned him,
“Teacher,” they said, “Moses said that if a man dies without children, his brother must marry his wife and raise up offspring for his brother.
Now there were seven brothers among us. The first married and died without offspring, so he left his wife to his brother.
The same happened to the second, and then the third, down to the seventh.
Finally, the woman also died.
So in the resurrection, whose wife will she be of the seven? For they all had her as a wife.”
Jesus answered, “You are mistaken, not knowing the Scriptures or the power of God.
For in the resurrection, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels of God in heaven.
But concerning the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what God said to you,
‘I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob’? He is not the God of the dead, but of the living.”

footnotes.content: When Jesus quotes, “I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob,” he draws from Exodus 3:6, where God speaks to Moses from the burning bush. The present tense “I am” signifies that these patriarchs, though physically dead, are alive to God, affirming the ongoing relationship between God and his covenant people beyond death. This declaration directly challenges the Sadducees' denial of the resurrection by revealing that God's promises to the patriarchs remain active, requiring their continued existence. It underscores the eternal nature of God's covenant and the certainty of resurrection, aligning divine identity with the living, not the dead.

text: When the crowds heard this, they were amazed at his teaching.
