When the Pharisees heard Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, they gathered together.
One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with a question:
“Teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the law?”
Jesus replied, “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind.’
This is the first and greatest commandment.
And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’
All the Law and the Prophets are founded on these two commandments.”
While the Pharisees were gathered together, Jesus asked them,
“What do you think about the Christ? Whose son is he?”
They replied, “The son of David.”
He said to them, “How then does David, speaking in the Spirit, call him ‘Lord,’ saying,
‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet”’?”

footnotes.content: Jesus' question, “If then David calls him ‘Lord,’ how can he be his son?” references Psalm 110:1, a key messianic passage. In Jewish thought, the Messiah was expected to be a descendant of David, a royal son who would restore Israel. However, by addressing the Messiah as “Lord,” David acknowledges a superior, divine authority, revealing a deeper mystery about the Messiah's identity. This implies that the Christ is not merely a human heir but also a divine figure, greater than David and preexistent in authority—a profound revelation that transcends mere lineage and points to the divine nature of the Messiah.

text: And no one could answer him, and from that day no one dared to question him anymore.

title: The Hypocrisy of the Scribes and Pharisees

text: Then Jesus spoke to the crowds and his disciples,
He said, “The scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses’ seat.
Therefore, do whatever they tell you, but do not follow their example, for they do not do what they teach you.
They tie up heavy burdens on people's shoulders, but will not lift a finger to help.
They do their deeds to be seen by others. They broaden their phylacteries and lengthen their tassels.
They treasure the places of honor at feasts and the best seats in the synagogues,
and greetings in the marketplaces, and to be called ‘Teacher’ in public.
But you are not to be called ‘Teacher,’ for you have only one teacher, the Christ, and you are all brothers and sisters.
And do not call anyone on earth your father, for you have only one Father; He who is in heaven.
Do not be called teachers, for you have one Teacher, the Christ.
Let the greatest among you be your servant.
For those who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted.
Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You shut the door of the kingdom of heaven in people's faces. You do not enter, nor do you let those entering go in.
Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You plunder the homes of widows and make long prayers as a pretense. For this, your judgment will be harsher.
Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You cross land and sea to make one convert, and when you succeed, you make them twice the child of Gehenna as yourselves.
Woe to you are you, blind guides! You deceive yourselves and others. You say, ‘Whoever swears by the temple, it means nothing; but whoever swears by the gold of the temple is bound by their oath.’
Blind fools! Which is greater, the gold or the temple that makes the gold sacred?
Blind guides! You say, ‘Whoever swears by the altar, it means nothing; but whoever swears by the gift on it is bound by their oath.’
You blind men! Which is greater, the gift, or the altar that makes the gift sacred?
Therefore, anyone who swears by the altar swears by it and everything on it.
And anyone who swears by the temple swears by it and by the one who dwells in it.
And anyone who swears by heaven swears by the throne of God and by the one who sits on it.

Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You tithe mint, anise, and cumin; but you neglect the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faithfulness. These you should have practiced, without neglecting the others.

footnotes.content: *These herbs represent small, easily measured tithes, contrasted with the weightier matters of the law like justice, mercy, and faithfulness. Jesus highlights their obsessive focus on minor rituals while neglecting core ethical principles.*

text: Blind guides! You strain out a gnat but swallow a camel.
Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside they are full of greed and self-indulgence.
Blind Pharisee! First clean the inside of the cup and dish, so that the outside may also be clean.
Woe to you are you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You are like whitewashed tombs; beautiful on the outside but full of dead bones and all manner of impurity within.

footnotes.content: *Whitewashing tombs was a common practice before Passover to prevent accidental ritual defilement. The imagery symbolizes external purity concealing internal decay and death, capturing the hypocrisy of outward righteousness without true inner transformation.*

text: In the same way, you appear righteous to others, but inside you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! You build the tombs of the prophets and decorate the monuments of the righteous.
And you say, ‘If we had lived in the days of our ancestors, we would not have joined them in shedding the blood of the prophets.’
So you testify against yourselves that you are descendants of those who murdered the prophets.
Fill up, then, the measure of your ancestors' guilt!
Serpents! Brood of vipers! How will you escape the coming judgment of Gehenna??
Therefore, I am sending you prophets, wise ones, and scribes. Some of them you will kill and crucify, others you will flog in your synagogues and pursue from town to town.

As a result, all the righteous blood shed on earth will come upon you, from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah son of Berekiah, whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar.
Truly, I tell you, all this will come upon this generation.

Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one who kills the prophets and stones those sent to her! How often I have wanted to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were not willing.
Behold, your house is abandoned to you in utter desolation.

footnotes.content: *The term 'house' oikos here can refer to the temple, the city of Jerusalem, or the broader covenant community. The desolation foretold reflects both the physical destruction of the temple (fulfilled in 70 AD) and the spiritual separation resulting from rejecting the Messiah.*

text: For I tell you, you will see me no more until you say, ‘Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.’

footnotes.content: *The phrase is a direct reference to Psalm 118:26, a messianic psalm often recited during the Passover. This line was famously shouted during Jesus' triumphal entry (Matthew 21:9) and is here used as a prophetic declaration of His eventual recognition as the Messiah at His return.*
